From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-05-13T08:45:00-07:00
Subject: Fwd: Questions on EU AI Act annex for pilot deployment

Team — forwarding Maria's request. Key asks: mapping runtime controls to EU obligations, regression cadence/process, and a traceability artifact. Priorities for the draft:

- One-page mapping table (control -> EU AI Act clause / NIST RMF alignment)
- Concrete regression plan (datasets, eval metrics, alerting thresholds, rollback policy)
- Redacted traceability log (sample showing request id, model version, KV-cache key id, latency/cost counters)

Constraints: Aurora will treat this as a near-term SOW attachment; procurement needs the matrix by next Tuesday ideally.

Jonas: can you provide the runtime control mapping (kernel selection, prefix caching, model variant fallbacks) and where we can claim automated fallback as a mitigation?
Neha: can you draft the regression testing cadence and monitoring thresholds and confirm any customer-configurable hooks?
Marissa: please assemble the assurance matrix doc and mock traceability sample; I'll handle coordinator with Aurora.

Thanks — Ben


From: Neha Kapoor <neha.kapoor@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-05-14T11:20:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Fwd: Questions on EU AI Act annex for pilot deployment

Folks — attaching the package I prepared with Marissa: 'deployment_assurance_matrix.xlsx' and 'eval-regression-plan.pdf'. Summary of the key points below (I'll paste into the matrix as well):

1) Mapping to EU AI Act / NIST AI RMF
- Traceability: We capture request-level metadata (request_id, timestamp, tenant_id, model_version, prompt_hash) and persist immutable inference logs to a customer-writable S3 bucket for Private deployments; for Hosted we provide streamed, signed log bundles and optional retention windows. This satisfies traceability obligations (Art. 13/Annex II mapping).
- Human Oversight: Our SDK supports response confidence scores and output provenance markers; customers can configure a 'human review' flag per-route that routes flagged responses to a configurable webhook. We document the oversight roles and escalate rules in the matrix.
- Accuracy Monitoring: We run nightly regression tests against a pinned eval set (customer can supply gold prompts). Metrics tracked: top-1 ROUGE-like correctness rate, hallucination incidence (heuristic), and latency P95. Thresholds are customer-configurable; default alerting fires if top-1 drops by >4% absolute or hallucination rate increases by >3% relative in a 7-day window.

2) Regression cadence & rollback
- Baseline: continuous evaluation in CI for any model or pipeline change.
- Nightly: full eval harness against production-weighted prompt set with differential checks vs. pinned production baseline.
- Canary: staged rollout with canary traffic for new model variants (default 5% for 24h) with automatic rollback on alert conditions.
- Audit trail: every deployment event stores artifacts (model commit hash, quantization profile, kernel config, batching profile, and eval snapshot IDs) linked to the traceability logs.

3) Traceability sample (redacted)
- We provide a redacted sample that shows: request_id = r_20260514_0001, model_version = rwd-7b-v2-quant, prompt_hash = h0a1b2c3, inference_time_ms = 72, output_confidence = 0.87, eval_snapshot = es_20260513_v1. The attached 'traceability-sample.log.txt' is redacted and includes the event sequence.

4) Customer hooks & artifacts we can hand off
- Signed assurance matrix (XLSX) mapping each clause to our control and evidence link
- Eval snapshots and diff reports (PDF) showing metric deltas
- Redacted inference trace (TXT) and optional full logs via secure transfer for Private deployments

Notes on claims: we avoid asserting guarantees about downstream application correctness; our deliverables are operational controls, monitoring, and automated rollback tooling. For compliance language in contracts we typically provide the evidence artifacts and a statement of operational controls scope.

Attachments: deployment_assurance_matrix.xlsx; eval-regression-plan.pdf; traceability-sample.log.txt

-Neha Kapoor
Head of Security & Compliance, Redwood Inference
neha.kapoor@redwood.ai

On 2026-05-13 08:45, Ben Carter wrote:
> Team — forwarding Maria's request.
> ...

title: Operational handbook: exit rights, notice windows & termination-for-convenience
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Tom Reynolds <t.reynolds@acme-cloud.com>, procurement@acme-cloud.com
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 16 May 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Operational handbook: exit rights, notice windows & termination-for-convenience

Tom / Acme procurement — thanks again for the productive call this morning. Attached is the redline we've been iterating on (ACME_MSA_redline_v3.docx). The main points we want to align on for the order form / MSA:

- Auto-renew mechanics: proposed 12-month initial term with annual auto-renew unless either party provides 60 days' written notice prior to renewal.
- Notice windows & effective dates: prefer notice to be tied to billing cycle (30/60/90 days) — proposal attached shows 60 days.
- Termination for convenience (T4C): Redwood is willing to include a limited T4C right on the order form with a 90-day notice and pro-rated termination liability limited to remaining pre-paid but non-refundable services.

We expect Acme Legal to push back on the language around \"pro-rated\" vs \"liquidated damages\" and whether convenience termination can be exercised in the middle of a committed capacity period. If useful, I've flagged the concession matrix in the summary spreadsheet (redline-summary.xlsx).

Next steps I propose: 
1) Acme legal provides annotated redline (we can accept comments in Word).
2) Redwood legal (Laura) to propose one clean fallback alternative for T4C and a short paragraph explaining the operational impact (billing & data handoff).
3) If redlines are narrow, run for internal exec approval and DocuSign. If more substantive, schedule a 30-minute legal sync.

Please confirm if 60 days for auto-renew is acceptable from Acme's perspective or if procurement needs 30 days to align with your PO processing.

Attachment: ACME_MSA_redline_v3.docx (Word redline)
Attachment: redline-summary.xlsx (xls)

Thanks,

Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | (415) 555-0123

[Attachment: ACME_MSA_redline_v3.docx (40KB)]
[Attachment: redline-summary.xlsx (12KB)]

From: Sarah Li <sli@acme-cloud.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, procurement@acme-cloud.com
Cc: Tom Reynolds <t.reynolds@acme-cloud.com>
Date: Sat, 16 May 2026 17:43:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Operational handbook: exit rights, notice windows & termination-for-convenience

Ben — thanks for sending. Attached are Acme's comments (inline in Word) and a short note from procurement: we cannot accept a T4C that allows termination without some measure to recoup onboarding costs for reserved capacity. Specific asks: 

1) If Redwood insists on a 90-day T4C, Acme requests a 6-month minimum notice for capacity-committed line items (or payment of unused committed capacity).
2) Auto-renew window: procurement prefers 30 days so our finance team has time to raise a PO.
3) Liquidated damages: Acme won't accept open-ended \"liability limited to pre-paid services\" language; propose either (a) a capped termination fee equal to 50% of remaining committed fees, or (b) a mutual termination-for-convenience with proportional refund calculations.

We added inline comments where the redline references \"committed pool\" and how billing credits would be returned. Also flagged data-handling obligations on termination (handoff timeframe, export format).

Attaching: ACME_MSA_comments_v1.docx

--
Sarah Li

- Auto-renew mechanics: proposed 12-month initial term with annual auto-renew unless either party provides 60 days' written notice prior to renewal.

- Termination for convenience (T4C): Redwood is willing to include a limited T4C right on the order form with a 90-day notice and pro-rated termination liability limited to remaining pre-paid but non-refundable services.

We expect Acme Legal to push back on the language around \"pro-rated\" vs \"liquidated damages\" and whether convenience termination can be exercised in the middle of a committed capacity period. If useful, I've flagged the concession matrix in the summary spreadsheet (redline-summary.xlsx).

Next steps I propose: 
1) Acme legal provides annotated redline (we can accept comments in Word).
2) Redwood legal (Laura) to propose one clean fallback alternative for T4C and a short paragraph explaining the operational impact (billing & data handoff).
3) If redlines are narrow, run for internal exec approval and DocuSign. If more substantive, schedule a 30-minute legal sync.

Please confirm if 60 days for auto-renew is acceptable from Acme's perspective or if procurement needs 30 days to align with your PO processing.

Attachment: redline-summary.xlsx (xls)

title: HeliosCorp RFP — Dedicated reservation capacity & cost-sensitivity Q&A
preview:
From: John Daly <john.daly@helioscorp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, procurement@helioscorp.com
Date: Tue, 12 May 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: RFP follow-up: dedicated reservation sizing details

Ben / Redwood team,

Thanks for the RFP response. Procurement needs additional clarity on your recommended GPU mix and how that maps to our stated throughput targets for the ML inference workloads. A few concrete asks so we can finalize the internal scoring: 

1) Per-tier throughput (tokens/sec and concurrent requests) for the recommended mix.
2) Latency SLOs (p50/p95/p99) at those throughputs.
3) Cost sensitivity: delta in monthly committed spend if we shift +/- 20% or +/- 50% of committed QPS.
4) Any constraints on mixed-model packing (e.g., can A100 and H100 be mixed on the same pool for the same reservation?).

We'd like a short worksheet and a one-pager with the sensitivity numbers. Can you get that to us by end of week?

John
John Daly
Head of Procurement, HeliosCorp
[Attachment: Helios_RFP_questions.pdf]

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: John Daly <john.daly@helioscorp.com>, procurement@helioscorp.com
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 12 May 2026 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: RFP follow-up: dedicated reservation sizing details

John — thanks for the clear list. We're on it. Quick summary of next steps from our side:

- I'll attach a committed-capacity workbook that maps your target QPS and average sequence lengths to a suggested GPU mix and estimated monthly cost.
- Karthik will run throughput p50/p95/p99 projections against your test trace and include a short note on packing constraints.
- Mina will draft the cost-sensitivity one-pager showing +/-20%/+/-50% commitment scenarios.

Deliverables: redwood_capacity_workbook_v2.xlsx, gpu_mix_matrix_v3.csv, cost_sensitivity_summary.pdf (draft) — target delivery: Fri EOD PT.

Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
[Attachment: redwood_capacity_workbook_v2.xlsx]

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 13 May 2026 11:22:00 -0700
Subject: Internal: HeliosCorp throughput assumptions and GPU-fit notes

Ben / Mina / Laura — notes from running Helios' sample trace (50k qps peak window, median seq len 32 tokens, tail 512):

- Baseline inference mix: ~70% chat-completion, 30% reranking/embeddings under the RFP assumptions.
- H100 (80GB) gives best p95 tail for long-context chat; A100 (40GB) is cost-efficient for embeddings and shorter completions.
- Packing: we recommend partitioning reservation pool by family for production SLOs (i.e., dedicated H100 pool and dedicated A100 pool). Mixed-model packing is supported at runtime but complicates strict latency guarantees unless you allow cross-family fallbacks.

Throughput projection (ballpark for the provided trace):
- Pool A (H100): handles 18k concurrent short-lived chat requests (median seq=32) => estimated p95 latency ~85ms, p99 ~220ms
- Pool B (A100): handles 32k embedding/rerank requests => estimated p95 latency ~60ms, p99 ~120ms

I'll include the calculation sheet and kernel-fit assumptions in gpu_mix_matrix_v3.csv. Ask: do they require single-SKU reservations, or are they open to a two-tier commit with optional burst credits? If two-tier accepted, cost sensitivity improves substantially.

-Karthik

From: John Daly <john.daly@helioscorp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement@helioscorp.com, Sarah Lin <sarah.lin@helioscorp.com>
Date: Thu, 14 May 2026 08:44:00 -0700
Subject: Re: RFP follow-up: dedicated reservation sizing details

Thanks — that breakdown helps. Procurement prefers a single reservation price, but we will consider a two-tier commit if Redwood can provide a deterministic fallback policy and an SLA for cross-pool fallback. Two quick clarifications: 

A) For the H100 p95/p99 numbers — are those measured at the per-token step or end-to-end response? We need end-to-end numbers for SLA purposes.
B) For the cost sensitivity, can you show monthly committed spend vs effective unit cost (per-1k tokens) under the three scenarios (baseline, -20%, +50%)?

If possible, include a short paragraph on rollback/fallback behavior when H100 capacity is exhausted.

John

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: John Daly <john.daly@helioscorp.com>, procurement@helioscorp.com
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 15 May 2026 16:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: RFP follow-up: dedicated reservation sizing details

John — attached are the updated items per your asks. Highlights below:

- Latency metrics are end-to-end (inference request -> final token emitted) using your trace; p50/p95/p99 included in the workbook by pool and by payload size. Example: H100 pool at planned commit (18k qps equivalent) => p95 end-to-end = 95ms, p99 = 260ms (long tails driven by 512-token requests).
- Cost sensitivity one-pager includes committed monthly spend and effective cost-per-1k-tokens for baseline, -20%, +50% commitment scenarios. Topline: moving from baseline -> -20% increases unit cost ~14%; baseline -> +50% decreases unit cost ~22% (assumes burst credits capped at 15% and autoscale headroom).
- Mixed-pool behavior: we support live fallback from H100 -> A100-compatible quantized variants. Fallback is policy-driven (latency-first or cost-first). For strict SLAs we recommend reserving a small buffer of compatible capacity (we show 8-12% buffer examples in the workbook).

Attachments: redwood_capacity_workbook_v2.xlsx (detailed workbook), gpu_mix_matrix_v3.csv (kernel & packing notes), cost_sensitivity_summary.pdf (one-pager).

Call to action: can we schedule a 30-min run-through early next week (Mon/Tue) to walk procurement + infra through the workbook?

Thanks,
Ben
[Attachment: gpu_mix_matrix_v3.csv][Attachment: cost_sensitivity_summary.pdf]

From: Sarah Lin <sarah.lin@helioscorp.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, John Daly <john.daly@helioscorp.com>
Cc: procurement@helioscorp.com
Date: Tue, 18 May 2026 15:41:00 -0700
Subject: Re: RFP follow-up: dedicated reservation sizing details

Ben — quick note from the procurement review: the workbook looks good. Two outstanding items before we finalize scoring: 

- Please add a short row in the workbook that shows the expected mean time-to-scale (cold start to ready) when bursting to spare capacity across AZs. Procurement wants to understand operational risk for cross-AZ bursts.
- Confirm whether KMS-backed keys can be scoped per-reservation (we need per-account key separation for audit).

If you can drop a brief reply with those two points and confirm the Mon/Tue run-through availability, we'll lock time and move to commercial terms.

Sarah
Senior Procurement Analyst, HeliosCorp

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Sarah Lin <sarah.lin@helioscorp.com>, John Daly <john.daly@helioscorp.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Mina Haddad <mina.haddad@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 18 May 2026 16:10:00 -0700
Subject: Re: RFP follow-up: dedicated reservation sizing details — quick clarifications

Sarah / John — answers inline:

- Mean time-to-scale for cross-AZ burst: typical warm-burst path (using pre-warmed VM images + cached model artifacts) is 45–90s to 95% of target capacity. Cold path (if images need to spin up from scratch and artifacts fetched) is 4–7 minutes. We include a recommended warm buffer (8–12%) in the workbook to avoid cold-path exposure.
- KMS per-reservation keys: yes — we support scope-limited KMS keys for each dedicated reservation in Private and Dedicated plans. We can show a sample KMS policy in the run-through and include it in the security appendix.

Mon/Tue run-through: Mon 2026-05-24 10:00am PT or Tue 2026-05-25 9:00am PT — pls confirm which works for your team and we'll send calendar invite.

Ben
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference — www.redwood.ai

Sarah / John — answers inline:

- Mean time-to-scale for cross-AZ burst: typical warm-burst path (using pre-warmed VM images + cached model artifacts) is 45–90s to 95% of target capacity. Cold path (if images need to spin up from scratch and artifacts fetched) is 4–7 minutes. We include a recommended warm buffer (8–12%) in the workbook to avoid cold-path exposure.
- KMS per-reservation keys: yes — we support scope-limited KMS keys for each dedicated reservation in Private and Dedicated plans. We can show a sample KMS policy in the run-through and include it in the security appendix.

I pulled your account and saw some early activity — would love to do a brief call to make sure a demo is a fit and to surface the right pilot advice. Before we schedule, two quick areas to help us tailor the session: 1) Primary use case: what are you most looking to build? (examples: live customer support summarization, search/embeddings, content generation for marketing, internal knowledge base) 2) Traffic: rough QPS or daily requests today and target after 3 months (ballpark is fine) 3) Timeline & priority: are you looking for a pilot in 2 weeks / 1 month / 2+ months? title: Follow-up: scope & budget confirmation — batching & concurrency assumptions
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Samir Rao <samir@NimbusHealth.com>
Cc: Elena Park <elena.park@nimbushealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 15 May 2026 14:12:00 -0700
Subject: Follow-up: scope & budget confirmation — batching & concurrency assumptions

Hi Samir / Elena —

Thanks again for a constructive discovery call today. Quick recap and the concrete asks we agreed to: actionable items first, then the assumptions we used so the econ model matches your expectations.

Action items (owner / ETA)
- Nimbus: confirm expected monthly token volume ranges and peak qps window (Samir / Tue 5/19)
- Redwood: provide a draft PoA (pricing + POC config) and batch/concurrency knobs for your workload (Ava / Mon 5/18) — attached draft proposal + token profile CSV
- Both: schedule a 30-min tech sync to validate KV/prefix reuse and latency SLOs (tentative Wed 5/20)

What we heard from your team during the call
- Core workload: conversational triage + summarization for clinical intake (short prompts, average response ~180 tokens).
- Expected steady-state traffic: 50–120 requests/sec (rps) with strong diurnal trough/peak (peak ~10:00–14:00 PT).
- Sample request composition: 70% single-turn generation (no long history), 30% multi-turn with small context reuse (~3 turns, ~350 token prefix average).
- Target unit-econ: $300–$500 per 1M tokens (goal is to land at or below $400/1M for production).

Assumptions I used for the draft pricing and POC config (please flag anything off):
- Average generated tokens per request: 180 tokens; average prompt tokens: 50 tokens => ~230 tokens total per request.
- Baseline qps at steady-state: 75 rps (avg), peak concurrency target 180 concurrent requests (short tail).
- Continuous batching: expect ~15–25ms batching windows on inference nodes with prefix reuse/KV cache hits for the 30% multi-turn.
- Typical batch sizes: 8–32 depending on instance and sequence length (we'll tune during POC).
- Latency SLO: p95 generation < 300ms.
- Month estimate: 50M–200M tokens (varies with pilot scale).

Because your target unit-econ is sub-$500/1M, we recommended the Dedicated pool (reserved capacity) with a mixing policy: primary dedicated for steady traffic + burst to shared hosted for spikes. That gets predictable cost while allowing you to meet the p95 latency target.

Please confirm the monthly token low/mid/high bands and the current procurement timeline so we can finalize the PoA.

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com | (415) 555-0124

Attachment stubs: poa-proposal-draft.pdf (pricing + config), nimbus_poc_token_profile.csv (sample token histogram)

To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>

Date: Fri, 15 May 2026 17:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Follow-up: scope & budget confirmation — batching & concurrency assumptions

Thanks Ava — useful recap. A few clarifications from our side:

1) Token volumes
- Pilot (month 1): ~12–18M tokens (we'll be routing ~10k–14k requests/day).
- Ramp (months 2–4): 40–75M tokens/month as usage spreads across clinics.
- Production target (6–12 months): 150–220M tokens/month if we onboard 60% of clinics.

2) Concurrency / qps profile
- Baseline rps: 20–30 rps across the fleet (global), but concentrated spikes to 110–180 rps during intake waves in the morning.
- Long tail: occasional bursts up to 300 rps for short periods (30–90s) during system-wide batch imports.

3) Latency tolerance
- Clinically acceptable p95: <350ms is OK for triage flows; <250ms preferred for clinician-facing summary screens.

4) Cost target
- We are aiming for an all-in production cost <= $350 / 1M tokens. For pilot we can accept a bit higher while we tune batching and caching.

We'll confirm the CSV with exact request-length histograms by Tue 5/19. Also, happy to take your PoA draft and iterate.

Best,
Samir
Head of Product, Nimbus Health
samir@NimbusHealth.com

Subject: internal: quick notes on config knobs for Nimbus POC

Ava / Monica — internal quick hit list before we send the tuned PoA: 

- Model choice: recommend a 13B-ish open variant with 8k context to balance latency vs cost. Will provide comparisons in the pricing sheet.
- Batching window: start with 20ms continuous batching on dedicated nodes; if KV hit rate > 30% we can tighten to 12–15ms.
- Cache policy: prefix-level KV caching for 30% multi-turn; expire 30m by default.
- Autoscale policy: target CPU/GPU usage 60–70% for steady capacity, with immediate burst to shared hosted for +30–50% spikes (keeps reserved cost smaller).
- Instrumentation: add token-level cost breakdown in console and a per-route p95 heatmap.

I'll attach a quick sim (separate doc) with cost per 1M tokens across batch sizes 8/16/32 for their token-length profile.

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.com>
To: Samir Rao <samir@NimbusHealth.com>
Cc: Elena Park <elena.park@nimbushealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 15 May 2026 14:12:00 -0700
Subject: Follow-up: scope & budget confirmation — batching & concurrency assumptions

Hi Samir / Elena —

Thanks again for a constructive discovery call today. Quick recap and the concrete asks we agreed to: actionable items first, then the assumptions we used so the econ model matches your expectations.

Action items (owner / ETA)
- Nimbus: confirm expected monthly token volume ranges and peak qps window (Samir / Tue 5/19)
- Redwood: provide a draft PoA (pricing + POC config) and batch/concurrency knobs for your workload (Ava / Mon 5/18) — attached draft proposal + token profile CSV
- Both: schedule a 30-min tech sync to validate KV/prefix reuse and latency SLOs (tentative Wed 5/20)

What we heard from your team during the call
- Core workload: conversational triage + summarization for clinical intake (short prompts, average response ~180 tokens).
- Expected steady-state traffic: 50–120 requests/sec (rps) with strong diurnal trough/peak (peak ~10:00–14:00 PT).
- Sample request composition: 70% single-turn generation (no long history), 30% multi-turn with small context reuse (~3 turns, ~350 token prefix average).
- Target unit-econ: $300–$500 per 1M tokens (goal is to land at or below $400/1M for production).

Assumptions I used for the draft pricing and POC config (please flag anything off):
- Average generated tokens per request: 180 tokens; average prompt tokens: 50 tokens => ~230 tokens total per request.
- Baseline qps at steady-state: 75 rps (avg), peak concurrency target 180 concurrent requests (short tail).
- Continuous batching: expect ~15–25ms batching windows on inference nodes with prefix reuse/KV cache hits for the 30% multi-turn.
- Typical batch sizes: 8–32 depending on instance and sequence length (we'll tune during POC).
- Latency SLO: p95 generation < 300ms.
- Month estimate: 50M–200M tokens (varies with pilot scale).

Because your target unit-econ is sub-$500/1M, we recommended the Dedicated pool (reserved capacity) with a mixing policy: primary dedicated for steady traffic + burst to shared hosted for spikes. That gets predictable cost while allowing you to meet the p95 latency target.

Please confirm the monthly token low/mid/high bands and the current procurement timeline so we can finalize the PoA.

Thanks —
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.com | (415) 555-0124

Attachment stubs: poa-proposal-draft.pdf (pricing + config), nimbus_poc_token_profile.csv (sample token histogram)

title: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>, procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T09:12:00Z
Subject: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Team / Sam,

Following our demo and sizing call last week, Velociti is verbally committing to a reserved GPU pool, but procurement asked for clarity on how we handle GPU market price swings in the contract. They want a disciplined approach to discounts/credits tied to market cost changes. I attached a draft SOW and a simple scenario workbook showing a few market moves.

Key asks for internal alignment:
- Recommended contract tenor (12 vs 24 months) and the commercial tradeoffs
- A simple contingency mechanism (e.g., tiered credits or monthly true-up) we can offer without exposing Redwood to open-ended downside
- Approval thresholds for discounts (who signs off at what % off list)

Sam / procurement: we want to give you a clear, implementable schedule so procurement can route approvals on their side. If helpful, we can propose a canary clause for the first 3 months.

Attachments: VEL-2317_Draft_SOW.pdf, GPU_Price_Scenarios.xlsx

Next steps: internal recommendations by EOD so I can send a proposal to Sam before EOD tomorrow.

Thanks,
Ava Chen
Head of Sales, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0192


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>, procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T10:03:00Z
Subject: Re: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Ava / team — quick thoughts:

1) Tenor recommendation: aim for a 12-month firm term with an optional 12-month extension at pre-agreed rates. That lowers our tail risk and gives Velociti runway.

2) Contingency mechanism (simple, cap-limited):
   - Define baseline GPU cost index (monthly average of public spot/wholesale price)
   - If index rises >20% vs baseline for two consecutive months -> customer receives a service credit equal to X% of monthly committed spend (propose 10%) capped at 2 months per 12-mo period
   - If index falls >20% -> we consider downward pass-through at pre-agreed tiers (helps procurement)

3) Approval thresholds: discounts/credits <=10% can be approved by Sales Director (Ava), >10% up to 20% requires Finance (Marcus) sign-off, >20% needs VP/exec approval.

I attached a template credit schedule we use with smaller customers — we can adapt. If Marcus can share the downside P&L hit at 10%/15%/20% that will help finalize thresholds.

-Karthik


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>, procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T12:27:00Z
Subject: Re: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Ava / Karthik — did a quick sensitivity using the attached commit_model_v3.csv (summarized below). Numbers are illustrative but directionally accurate:

- Baseline: committed monthly spend = $180k (Velociti sizing)
- 10% market price increase -> incremental cost ~ $18k/month (we'd absorb vs credit if not capped)
- 10% credit to customer equals $18k; at 3 months that is $54k (material)

Recommendation:
- Keep credit cap per 12-mo window (e.g., 2 months max) and limit single-event exposure (prevents blowups if market spikes).
- Use a tiered approach (10% credit for 20–30% price move; 15% credit for 30–50% move; >50% -> automatic review + exec approval).

Operational note: we need a clear index provider and a monthly reconciliation process (finance + billing). Without that we can't reconcile credits reliably. I can own the reconciliation design but need product/ops commitment.

Attaching commit_model_v3.csv for the numbers. Approve routing rules and I'll break out P&L scenarios for legal/contract.

-Marcus


From: Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T13:05:00Z
Subject: Re: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Ava / all,

Thanks for the quick turn. Procurement needs a deterministic schedule they can include in their purchase order. Our preference is:

- 12-month firm term + 12-month option
- A clear discount/credit table tied to a public GPU cost index (we use vendorSpotIndex.com)
- Cap and trigger definitions so our CFO can sign off

If you can send a redlineable table and a one-paragraph explanation of the reconciliation process, procurement can route legal and finance in parallel. We can also sign a time-limited canary for the first quarter if Redwood wants to limit long-term exposure.

Sam Rios
Head of Infrastructure, Velociti AI
sam@velociti.ai


From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Sam Rios <sam@velociti.ai>, procurement@velociti.ai
Date: 2026-05-18T15:02:00Z
Subject: Re: Reserved capacity tenor & GPU cost contingency — Velociti AI

Thanks everyone — good alignment. Action items I will take this afternoon:

1) Draft a one-page contingency table (tiered triggers, caps, index source) and attach as an exhibit to the SOW.
2) Propose approval flow: Ava (Sales) up to 10%; Marcus (Finance) 10–20%; Laura (Legal) looped for >15% language; VP Sales for anything >20%.
3) Share the redlineable SOW + exhibit with Sam and procurement by 09:00 PT tomorrow.

Marcus: please send the P&L scenarios by EOD so I can include the recommended caps. Karthik: please send the small-customer template if you still have it.

Will mark this as high-priority — thank you.

Regards,
Ava Chen
Head of Sales, Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai
+1 (415) 555-0192

From: Alex Zhou <azhou@acme.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>
Cc: procurements@acme.ai
Date: 2026-05-17 09:06 PDT
Subject: Dedicated capacity ask for Q3 pilot

Hi Camila —

Thanks again for the demo last week. We want to move the pilot to a dedicated capacity model for Q3 so we can hit interactive SLOs for our B2B product. Attached is a high-level usage forecast and the procurement email for our finance team.

Quick questions to help us lock timing:
- What minimum slot count do you recommend for a 10k DAU-ish pilot with 95th p95 token latency < 250ms?
- Any constraints around start date (we'd like July 6 start, 3-month initial commitment)?

If helpful, procurement can loop in a contract request.

Appreciate a quick sizing note so we can budget.

—Alex
Head of Product, Acme AI

From: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>

Date: 2026-05-17 10:12 PDT
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity ask for Q3 pilot

Hi Alex —

Thanks — received the forecast. I copied Laura and Ben to get alignment on capacity and finance cadence, and Karthik to confirm infra constraints. Preliminary read: the shape looks bursty around peak hours (US PST). We can support the July 6 start if we commit now to a slot reservation and confirm the committed QPS profile.

Proposed immediate steps:
1) Ben/Infra to provide a conservative slot recommendation for the pilot (target p95 latency <=250ms).
2) Finance (Laura) to confirm billing cadence and required contract terms for a 3-month reserved window.
3) Camila to send a one-page mutual action plan and SOW draft.

I'll loop in Jonas for slot orchestration if we need to carve out elastic burst capacity.

Best,
Camila Reyes
Senior Account Executive — Redwood Inference
Redwood Inference

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.ai>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-05-17 12:08 PDT
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity ask for Q3 pilot — initial slot estimate

Camila / team —

I pulled the forecast and did a quick model assuming: 10k DAU, 30 requests/user/day, average 120 tokens/request, peak concurrency factor 8x. Conservative recommendation: 6 reserved slots (each slot ~= 90 concurrent streams on our XG-1 profile) with an additional 2 burst slots for weekend/marketing spikes. That would give expected p95 token latency ~220ms for the workload shape in the CSV.

Notes / risks:
- If Acme pushes higher prompt length (>=300 tokens avg) we should +2 reserved slots.
- Weekday evening spikes (20:00-22:00 PST) drive most of the concurrency; prefix caching might help reduce token costs but won't remove peak compute needs.

Actionable ask for sales: confirm expected average tokens and whether batch inference will be used for any portion of the workload.

Ben
Head of Capacity Planning
Redwood Inference

Attachment referenced: internal_slot_model_v1.xlsx (internal)

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.ai>
To: Camila Reyes <camila@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.ai>, Jonas Weber <jonas@redwood.ai>
Date: 2026-05-17 14:55 PDT
Subject: Re: Dedicated capacity ask for Q3 pilot — infra constraints & lead times

Quick update from infra:
- We have a maintenance window in the July 4-7 range for a subset of XG-1 pools but we can prioritize allocation to Acme's slot group if terms are signed by June 10.
- Lead time for bringing 2 additional burst slots online (if we need them pre-start) is ~9 business days for procurement and validation.
- Networking: for private telemetry we need an IP allowlist; Acme's security team should provide CIDR blocks before provisioning (Laura, Camila can help coordinate).

Recommendation: sign LOI / SOW and lock commit by June 5 to hit July 6 ramp with 6 reserved + 2 burst. If signing slips we still can start with 4 reserved but will risk p95 degradations during peak until burst capacity is validated.

Karthik
Director, Infrastructure Operations
Redwood Inference

Date: 2026-05-12T11:52:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: performance evaluation, results & close timeline for ACME Health

Ben —

Thanks for the MAP. We kicked off the Locust run this morning and I attached the raw output (acme_locust_results_0509.zip). Quick summary of what we saw vs your targets:
- Median TTFT: ~320 ms
- p95: ~790 ms
- Peak throughput (short burst): ~95 req/s
- Test config: Locust 2.18, 500 users spawn, target concurrency ~8 active sessions, sequence length 256 tokens average.

Notes / things that might explain divergence:
- Our test harness does not enable persistent KV caching between requests (we run stateless test users).
- We are running through an internal proxy that adds an extra network hop; I can get hop details from infra if you want.
- We saw occasional 5xx spikes under burst load (logs included).

We can run a second pass with KV caching enabled and a run without the proxy to help isolate. We'd also appreciate if Redwood can reproduce with our exact Locust config.

Samir

> Ben —

From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Samir Gupta <samir.gupta@acmehealth.com>
Date: 2026-05-14T09:37:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: performance evaluation, results & close timeline for ACME Health

Ben / team / Samir —

Attached are our reproduction results (repro_results_20260512.csv) and a short side-by-side summary (redwood_side_by_side_05014.pdf). High level findings from the repros run in our staging cluster using the ACME Locust profile:

- Redwood repro (match Locust config, KV caching OFF): median TTFT 310 ms, p95 720 ms, throughput 105 req/s at concurrency 8. Numbers are close to ACME's initial run, so harness parity looks good.
- Redwood repro (KV caching ON, persistent pins): median TTFT 150 ms, p95 420 ms, throughput 215 req/s at concurrency 8. Enabling prefix/KV caching cuts median ~160ms and p95 ~300ms in our tests.
- Competitive comparison: we tested a public competitor (CompetitorX v2 FP16 with kernel fusion) and saw median 140 ms, p95 390 ms. Important differentiation: competitor uses an FP16 fused kernel which reduces token compute but also relaxes model output determinism.

Probable causes for ACME's higher p95 on the initial run:
1) KV/prefix caching not enabled in Locust profile (this aligns with delta between our two runs).
2) Extra proxy/network hop increasing tail latency during bursts.
3) Locust client-side pacing adds bursts that interact poorly with queueing/batching — recommend steady arrival rate for p95 validation.

Actionable items we recommend for the MAP and the Thursday debug call:
- ACME: run a short 20-minute test with KV caching ON and without the proxy; attach results.
- Redwood: prepare a canary config that enables caching + conservative batching thresholds and share the rollback plan.
- Both: agree on p95 gate and monitoring hooks for the canary (synthetic cadence + sampled traces).

Attachments: repro_results_20260512.csv, redwood_side_by_side_05014.pdf

Rishi Malhotra
Senior Applied ML Engineer, Redwood Inference
rishi.malhotra@redwood.com

title: Allocator deadlock unwind — evidence package and next steps
preview:
From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwoodinference.com>
To: Asha Rao <asha@orbitech.com>, ops@orbitech.com
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2026-05-12T08:03:00Z
Subject: Allocator deadlock unwind — evidence package and next steps

Hi Asha / OrbitTech ops team,

First, I want to apologise for the disruption you saw this morning between 02:10–04:45 UTC when a subset of your reserved GPU pool experienced elevated queue depth and stalled allocations. We understand this impacted request latencies and a small percentage of timed-out requests for a portion of your traffic.

What we have for you in this message: a concise timeline, the evidence package (logs + traces), what we did to restore service, and our next steps including a proposed credit calculation. I've attached the logs and a credit worksheet, and added a Drive folder with the full evidence bundle.

Summary (short):
- Incident window: 2026-05-12 02:10–04:45 UTC (partial impact; autoscaler failed to add warm nodes)
- Root cause (short): allocator deadlock triggered by a concurrent warmup + eviction path race that held pool locks and prevented the allocator from completing node provisioning flows
- Customer impact: elevated latencies; ~1.8% of requests experienced timeouts above your SLA threshold during peak minutes
- Mitigation performed: isolated the offending allocator path and forced a controlled unwind of warmup state at 03:05 UTC, drained stuck allocations, and rolled a hotfix at 04:00 UTC to unblock new provisioning requests
- Next actions: permanent fix to allocator lock ordering, debounce in autoscaler, improved metrics/alerting, formal postmortem and credit proposal

Attachments: allocator_logs_20260512.tar.gz (trimmed logs + traces), sla_credit_calc_OrbitTech.xlsx (proposed credit math)
Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c-unwind-evidence
Jira: OPS-4321 (tracking remediation)

Can we schedule a 30-minute sync tomorrow so Marissa can walk through the timeline and we can validate the credit calc with your finance/ops team? Proposed slots: 2026-05-13 15:00 UTC or 2026-05-13 18:00 UTC. Let me know which works and we'll send an invite.

Again — apologies for the disruption. We take this seriously and are prioritizing both the technical fix and a clear commercial reconciliation.

Best,
Chloe Martin
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
chloe.martin@redwoodinference.com
+1 (415) 555-0192



From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwoodinference.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Asha Rao <asha@orbitech.com>, ops@orbitech.com
Date: 2026-05-12T12:28:00Z
Subject: Re: Allocator deadlock unwind — evidence package and next steps

Chloe / team / Asha,

Adding the technical timeline and immediate actions we took so OrbitTech has the specifics for ops review.

Technical timeline (engineer view):
- 02:09:35 UTC — autoscaler received warmup request for new nodes as token pressure increased.
- 02:10:12 UTC — concurrent eviction sweep kicked in on same pool shard. The eviction path attempted to acquire pool metadata lock while warmup flow held a different, dependent lock. Under certain interleavings a circular wait occurred between warmup <-> eviction <-> allocator-lease steps.
- 02:12–03:05 UTC — allocator goroutines moved into a stuck state; provisioning requests queued in the request dispatcher. Metrics showed node provisioning attempts stalled while existing nodes had marginal headroom.
- 03:05:10 UTC — we executed a controlled unwind command to forcibly release warmup state for the affected shard; this allowed the allocator to resume and start provisioning previously queued requests.
- 03:40:00 UTC — throughput returned to baseline; we rolled a targeted binary patch to reorder lock acquisition and added a scoped timeout in the eviction path.
- 04:00:00 UTC — hotfix fully deployed to the cluster; autoscaler debounce knobs adjusted to avoid simultaneous warmup+eviction when shard headroom is below threshold.

Immediate mitigations already applied:
- Controlled unwind (manual) to break the deadlock
- Scoped hotfix with lock ordering change and eviction timeout
- Autoscaler debounce change (temporary) to avoid triggering both paths concurrently
- Alerting: new high-priority metric (allocator_provision_stalls) and a runbook for manual unwind if seen again

Planned permanent work (tracked on OPS-4321):
- Formal locking refactor to remove circular dependency (ETA 4 weeks)
- Autoscaler state machine improvement to serialize warmup vs eviction when concurrency would risk headroom starvation (ETA 3 weeks)
- Add full integration test that fuzzes concurrent warmup/eviction flows (ETA 2 weeks)

Visible evidence in the attached bundle: trimmed allocator stack traces for stuck goroutines, provisioning request traces showing queued timestamps, and the patch diff for the hotfix. If you want raw traces or an expanded window, we can drop those into the Drive folder.

Happy to present these details in the sync — I can share terminal traces live and walk the lock stack.

-Marissa
Marissa Cole
Senior SRE, Redwood Inference
marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com

Hi Asha / OrbitTech ops team,

First, I want to apologise for the disruption you saw this morning between 02:10–04:45 UTC when a subset of your reserved GPU pool experienced elevated queue depth and stalled allocations. We understand this impacted request latencies and a small percentage of timed-out requests for a portion of your traffic.

What we have for you in this message: a concise timeline, the evidence package (logs + traces), what we did to restore service, and our next steps including a proposed credit calculation. I've attached the logs and a credit worksheet, and added a Drive folder with the full evidence bundle.

Summary (short):
- Incident window: 2026-05-12 02:10–04:45 UTC (partial impact; autoscaler failed to add warm nodes)
- Root cause (short): allocator deadlock triggered by a concurrent warmup + eviction path race that held pool locks and prevented the allocator from completing node provisioning flows
- Customer impact: elevated latencies; ~1.8% of requests experienced timeouts above your SLA threshold during peak minutes
- Mitigation performed: isolated the offending allocator path and forced a controlled unwind of warmup state at 03:05 UTC, drained stuck allocations, and rolled a hotfix at 04:00 UTC to unblock new provisioning requests
- Next actions: permanent fix to allocator lock ordering, debounce in autoscaler, improved metrics/alerting, formal postmortem and credit proposal

Attachments: allocator_logs_20260512.tar.gz (trimmed logs + traces), sla_credit_calc_OrbitTech.xlsx (proposed credit math)
Drive: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1a2b3c-unwind-evidence
Jira: OPS-4321 (tracking remediation)

Can we schedule a 30-minute sync tomorrow so Marissa can walk through the timeline and we can validate the credit calc with your finance/ops team? Proposed slots: 2026-05-13 15:00 UTC or 2026-05-13 18:00 UTC. Let me know which works and we'll send an invite.

Again — apologies for the disruption. We take this seriously and are prioritizing both the technical fix and a clear commercial reconciliation.

Hi Olivia / Jake —

Thanks again for choosing Redwood for the evaluation. To keep the session efficient for both your security team and our engineers, we propose a two-hop walkthrough: a short executive overview followed by a separate technical deep-dive. High level plan: 

- Exec: 30 minutes — objectives, data flows, compliance posture, Q&A (suits risk/compliance leads)
- Tech: 60 minutes — architecture, controls, pen-test/segmentation details, evidence handoff (suits engineering/security)

Suggested slots (all in CET):

- Option 2: Wed 19 May — Exec 15:00–15:30, Tech 15:45–16:45

title: Findly pilot: relevancy / reranker pilot checklist & next steps
preview:
From: Claire Huang <claire@findly.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: Sam Patel <sam.patel@findly.com>
Date: Tue, 12 May 2026 09:15:00 -0700
Subject: Relevancy pilot — notes + data ask

Ava / team — thanks for the demo this morning. Quick recap from our side and a few asks so your engs can scope the pilot: 

- Goal: replace heuristic reordering in our search results with a lightweight reranker for top 50 candidates; target improvement: +10–15% NDCG@10 and measurable reduction in user re-clicks.
- Data: we can provide ~2.5M query -> candidate lists (JSONL) with click labels for a rolling 90d window. Average candidates per query ~120, median 48.
- Security: we need an overview of how Redwood handles PII in logs, where embeddings are stored, and whether keys can be scoped to a VPC/private deployment.
- Integration asks (high level): SDK vs HTTP direct; auth options; support for structured output / tool-calling if we want to attach reranker signals back into our orchestration.

Attached: Findly_Rank_POC_requirements.pdf — sketch of data schema and acceptance criteria.

Next steps: could you share a short checklist of what your eng team will need (auth, sample ingestion path, routing for candidate generation vs rerank call, eval harness)? Also copying infra (infra@findly.com) — they had a couple of low-level questions on allowed IPs for dedicated endpoints.

Thanks,
Claire Huang
Head of Search, Findly

---


From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Claire Huang <claire@findly.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 12 May 2026 11:00:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Relevancy pilot — notes + data ask

Hi Claire — great call, appreciate the detail. High-level replies and an initial engineer checklist below; Ben/rishi/kim will scope and follow up with a proposed timeline.

Quick answers:
- Pembedding storage / PII: we support customer-controlled key management (KMS) for hosted and Private deployments; we can provide SOC 2 evidence and a short whitepaper on retention controls.
- Auth: API keys for quick POC, JWT/OIDC for integrations, and mTLS available for Dedicated/Private — we’ll include sample config.
- SDK vs HTTP: our SDK wraps retry/backoff, streaming, and structured output parsing; direct HTTP is fine for simple calls but SDK will speed up dev time.

Engineer checklist (what we’ll need from Findly):
1) Sample JSONL (2k rows) with candidate lists + click labels for offline eval.
2) Volume estimate (QPS) and peak token-length expectations.
3) Which fields you want returned with structured output / metadata (e.g., reranker_score, provenance_id).
4) Allowed IP ranges for any dedicated endpoints (if infra needs IP allowlisting).
5) Contact for infra + SRE for network details.

Ben/Rishi — would you pick this up and propose a pilot runbook (scoping, infra, eval metric baseline) by EOD Thu? Kimberly — can you propose acceptance criteria aligned to Findly’s product KPIs?

Regards,
Ava Chen
Enterprise Sales — Redwood Inference

On Tue, May 12, 2026 at 09:15 Claire Huang <claire@findly.com> wrote:
> Ava / team — thanks for the demo this morning. Quick recap from our side and a few asks so your engs can scope the pilot: ...

---


From: Claire Huang <claire@findly.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Findly Infra <infra@findly.com>, Sam Patel <sam.patel@findly.com>
Date: Wed, 13 May 2026 08:28:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Relevancy pilot — notes + data ask

Thanks Ava — infra contact (copied) is infra@findly.com. A couple of clarifications from our engineers: 

- Data size confirmation: the 2.5M queries is correct; we can export a 2k-row sample for you by EOD.
- Candidate generation: we currently return ~120 candidates from our retrieval step; for the pilot we plan to keep the same generator and call a reranker for top 50.
- Evaluation metrics: we will compute offline MRR, NDCG@10, and also run pairwise preference judgments for a ~500-query human eval tranche. Curious whether Redwood has recommended baselines and tooling for computing these (NDCG variants, bootstrap CI, A/B gating).
- Top_k behavior: we expect to test k in {10, 20, 50} and measure latency/cost tradeoffs; what's a typical latency impact for your neural reranker at top50?

Claire

On Tue, May 12, 2026 at 11:00 Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Claire — great call, appreciate the detail. High-level replies and an initial engineer checklist below; Ben/rishi/kim will scope and follow up with a proposed timeline.

---


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Claire Huang <claire@findly.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 13 May 2026 10:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Relevancy pilot — notes + data ask

Claire / team — looping in a short technical plan and recommended defaults from the Redwood side. I'll attach a short playbook.

Top-level architecture (recommended):
- Candidate-gen service (Findly) => pass top-50 candidates to Redwood Reranker endpoint.
- Option A (fastest to POC): direct HTTP POST to Redwood ranking API, using API key scoped to a project; SDK not required but recommended.
- Option B (production): SDK + JWT/OIDC + regional routing to a Dedicated pool (if you need consistent latency).

Auth and networking:
- POC: API key in header (x-api-key). We log only metadata (request size/tokens); payload retention can be turned off per team.
- Production: OIDC tokens with short TTL + audience-based routing; mTLS for Dedicated endpoints.
- IP allowlist: we can provide stable egress IPs for Dedicated; for Hosted we recommend using region-based routing and fallback.

Reranker performance notes:
- At top_k=10: add ~8–12ms per request per token on an A100-like pod with our optimized kernels (depends on sequence length).
- At top_k=50: expect 3–4x increase in per-call compute vs top_k=10; batching helps (we can batch multiple rerank calls if QPS justifies).
- Cost estimate: we’ll model cost based on average candidate length and request rate; Ben will share a simple cost sheet.

Evaluation and metrics:
- Offline: recommend computing NDCG@10, MRR, and hit-rate@k. Use bootstrap CIs (1k resamples) for significance.
- Online: canary A/B with Bayesian bandit or fixed-split; we recommend starting with a 5% traffic canary for 2 weeks with predefined success criteria.

Suggested pilot run (milestones):
1) Week 0–1: sample ingest + offline baseline (Findly provides 2k sample)
2) Week 2: deploy POC reranker (top50) + offline eval & tuning (k sweep)
3) Week 3–4: 5% canary with telemetry (latency, NDCG delta, re-click rate)

Attachments: redwood_rerank_playbook.md (short ops checklist); I'll share the cost sheet separately.

Ben Carter
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com

On Wed, May 13, 2026 at 08:28 Claire Huang <claire@findly.com> wrote:
> Thanks Ava — infra contact (copied) is infra@findly.com. A couple of clarifications from our engineers: ...

---


From: Rishi Malhotra <rishi@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 14 May 2026 15:22:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Relevancy pilot — notes + data ask

Forwarding my quick internal notes after reading Claire's infra questions and Ben's plan.

- Action items for this week:
  * Ben: upload redwood_rerank_playbook.md and cost_estimate.xlsx to the shared Drive link and flag any assumptions.
  * Ava: confirm PO and legal contact for a short POC MSA (we'll aim for a 30d eval window).
  * Kimberly: draft acceptance criteria mapping to NDCG delta / re-click reduction and add guardrails for latency increase (no more than +60ms p50).
- Pilot timeline: Ben to spin up a POC endpoint (hosted) by Mon next week; we’ll run offline eval and propose k sweep (10/20/50).

Small note on metrics: for the initial human eval tranche we recommend at least 500 queries with pairwise judgments; that will give a reasonable signal when combined with offline NDCG and online canary.

Rishi Malhotra
Senior ML Infra, Redwood Inference

preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Nguyen <alex@carecohealth.com>, Priya Shah <priya.shah@carecohealth.com>

Alex / Priya,

Thanks again for taking the time today — great conversation. Quick recap and asks so we can put together a concrete pricing / payback model for the CareCo pilot: 

1) Primary feature scope: summarize — clinical triage assistant for intake + automated discharge summaries (expected to be rolled out to 3 clinics in Q3).
2) Volume assumptions we heard today: ~200k user events/month translating to ~5M tokens/month initially, with heavy tail growth to ~20M tokens/month in 12 months. Please confirm whether the 5M/month is conservative or representative.
3) Target economics: you mentioned a target of $2–$3k per 1M tokens for production (including any discounts for committed volumes). We need your target $/1M token range so we can map to feature-level unit economics.
4) Key business metrics you want tied to the model: CAC payback target (months), feature-level gross margin target vs inference-only cost, and acceptable latency (p95 sub-800ms per request).

What I'll do next: Marissa will model three scenarios (pilot conservative, target growth, aggressive scale) with $/1M token sensitivity and the resulting $/feature and CAC payback curves. Connor will prepare a draft SOW + committed volume pricing band we can use for procurement.

Attachments: please see referenced token estimate template (we'll fill in with your confirmed numbers).

Can you confirm by EOD Tuesday: the 5M tokens/month baseline and the desired CAC payback horizon (in months)? Also attach any internal SLA/latency requirements or procurement timing constraints.

Thanks — talk soon.

Ben

Enterprise Sales | Redwood Inference

Ben — thanks for the summary and for looping Marissa / Connor in. Quick answers below: 

- Baseline tokens: 5M tokens/month is what we modeled for Q3 once ingest pipelines are live — that's representative for pilot (3 clinics). We expect a conservative adoption curve so treat that baseline as "low" rather than optimistic.
- 12-month view: ~18–22M tokens/month if we push across more of our network, so your growth scenarios are aligned.
- Target $/1M tokens: we'd like to see scenarios at $3k, $2k, and $1.5k per 1M tokens so we can understand thresholds. Procurement will push back on anything above $3k unless there's a clear ROI.

title: POC ownership, success criteria & decision escrow (post-call recap)
preview:
From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>
To: Ethan Cole <ethan.cole@stellarfin.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
Cc: 
Date: Mon, 4 May 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: POC ownership, success criteria & decision escrow (post-call recap)

Hi Ethan / team,

Thanks again for the call this morning — quick recap and proposed next steps so we have a single source of truth. Please review and confirm owners where noted.

High-level objective:
- Validate that Redwood Inference can meet Stellar Financial's latency & throughput needs for your batch scoring and nearline chat assistant use cases, while maintaining auditability and data residency controls.

Proposed success criteria (short list):
1) Performance: average 95th pctl latency for chat < 250ms for 512-token requests in our staging traffic shape.
2) Throughput: sustained 1.5M tokens/day during peak batch windows without >5% error rate.
3) Quality: top-line prompt eval set F1 within 6% of baseline model (as agreed in eval doc).
4) Compliance: data ingestion and audit logs for trial are retained for 90 days and accessible to Stellar security team.

Evaluation plan & timeline (draft):
- Week 0 (this week): finalize SOW & RACI. (ACTION: Ethan confirm procurement contact)
- Week 1: infra onboarding + private VPC network handshake (Redwood infra lead + Stellar cloud ops)
- Weeks 2–3: sample data ingestion, prefix cache validation, baseline evals
- Weeks 4–5: stress/throughput run, latency tuning & quantization checks
- Week 6: handoff readout + decision (go/no-go)

Owner asks (proposed RACI entries):
- Infra (VPC, networking, capacity): Redwood Dedicated eng — OWNER: Ben Carter (Redwood) / RACI: Ben - R/A, Connor - C, Ethan - I
- App integration (client SDKs, ingestion pipeline): Stellar app eng — OWNER: Ethan Cole / RACI: Ethan - R/A, Kimberly - C, Connor - C
- Security & compliance (data residency, audit logs, evidence): Stellar security — OWNER: Sophia Martinez / RACI: Sophia - R/A, Mina - C, Ava - I
- Procurement / contracts: Stellar procurement — OWNER: procurement@stellarfin.com / RACI: procurement - R/A, Ben - C

title: MAP: Ramp & Pacing Playbook for Final Performance Eval
preview:
From: Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: platform@sage-analytics.com
Date: 2026-05-05T09:15:00-07:00
Subject: MAP: final perf eval — clarifying pacing expectations

Hi Ava,

Thanks again for the walkthrough yesterday. As we finalize the MAP for the POC close, the platform team asked for a short playbook that defines the ramp/pacing profiles we'll run and the pass/fail gating for TTFT, sustained throughput, and queue length under load. They want to understand how Redwood will validate burst vs steady-state behavior and any recommended rate-limit tuning to avoid head-of-line blocking.

Can you share a proposed schedule (dates/times), the exact ramp scripts you plan to run, and the acceptance thresholds your team will use? We need this to commit the final sign-off on 5/12.

TIA — Emma

Emma Price
Director, Platform Engineering | Sage Analytics
[Attachment: spec-snapshot.pdf (PDF)]


From: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
To: Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-05T11:03:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: final perf eval — clarifying pacing expectations

Emma,

Appreciate the clear ask — attaching a compact playbook and ramp script. Summary here so it's in the thread:

Planned window: 2026-05-06 09:00–13:00 PT (warmup + 3 profiles) and 2026-05-07 10:00–14:00 PT (regression and final acceptance).
Profiles:
- Spike profile (sawtooth bursts): 30s ramp to 8x baseline QPS, hold 60s, drop to baseline; repeated 12x over 40 minutes to stress concurrency and observe queue shedding.
- Ramp-and-hold (steady-state): linear ramp from baseline to target over 5 minutes, hold for 30 minutes at each target (targets = 1x, 2x, 4x, 6x baseline throughput).
- Micro-burst series: 5 x 10s microbursts at 12x baseline to observe head-of-line blocking and KV-cache thrash.

Key metrics we will capture (token-level where possible):
- TTFT (time-to-first-token) median, p95, p99 by model & route
- Token/sec throughput (observed) and accepted QPS (ingress)
- Service queue depth and per-worker pending requests
- Error rate and 429s; origin of 429 (ingress vs internal queue drop)
- Cold vs warmed KV-cache behavior via prefix-hit tagging

Acceptance gating (pass/fail):
- TTFT: p95 <= 200ms, p99 <= 500ms for steady-state at the committed target; allowance for transient spikes during 30s bursts but p99 <= 800ms for bursts.
- Sustained throughput: observed token/sec within +/-15% of target for steady-hold windows (30m) without sustained queue growth.
- Queueing: service queue depth must stabilize after warmup; no sustained increase > 50% baseline for more than 5 minutes.

Rate-limit recommendations: windowed throttling at ingress per-customer with a short token-bucket to smooth microbursts; add an adaptive lane that allows brief overcommit for warmup but drops large bursts that cause queue blowups. We'll include exact bucket settings in ramp_script.yaml.

Attaching: ramp_script.yaml, pacing_profile_definitions.md, queueing_observability-checklist.pdf.

If the gate thresholds look OK, we'll lock the schedule and run a dry-run the morning of 5/6.

Thanks,
Ava

Ava Chen
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
[Attachment: ramp_script.yaml (YAML)]

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-05T12:10:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: final perf eval — clarifying pacing expectations

Quick technical notes from runtime side:

1) For burst profiles we should enable per-worker request queue caps and early drop with a distinct 429 code so we can separate ingress limiter hits from internal pressure drops. We'll tag dropped requests for traceability.
2) Set KV-cache warming: pre-seed prefixes for the longest prompt sets to reduce cold-TTFT variance. I'll attach a short snippet for the loader.
3) Observability: ensure token-level timings are exported to the tracing backend and we capture per-model kernel-switch counts — that'll help debug outliers.

Also: during the first spike run let's keep autoscaler off for deterministic results; we'll re-enable on 5/07 for end-to-end check.

-Karthik

[Attachment: kv_warm_loader.py (PY)]

From: Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Cc: platform@sage-analytics.com
Date: 2026-05-06T08:02:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: final perf eval — clarifying pacing expectations

Thanks — schedule looks good. Two quick asks before the dry-run:
1) Can you add a short column in the results CSV that maps each sample to the MAP milestone (e.g., \"warmup\", \"spike-1\", \"hold-4\").
2) For the rate-limit tuning: can you propose two sets of token-bucket params (conservative and aggressive) so we can discuss tradeoffs with security/procurement?

We'll be ready for the dry-run at 09:00 PT.

—Emma


From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
To: Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-06T10:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: final perf eval — clarifying pacing expectations

Emma/Team — following the dry-run we've uploaded preliminary results to the shared drive and a compact CSV (pacing-results-20260507.csv). Observations:

- Dry-run spike series produced transient p99 TTFT ~740ms with a single 8-minute window where queue depth trended up; root cause was ingress burst smoothing not applied on their generator.
- Steady-state holds (2x and 4x) met the steady gating: p95 TTFT 120–180ms, p99 320–420ms, observed token/sec within +/-10% of target.
- Micro-bursts exposed a head-of-line effect for 1% of requests tied to an older kernel path (we flagged and pinned those workers in the run).

Actions:
- We'll apply the conservative token-bucket (params in the attached) and re-run the spike suite tomorrow 10:00 PT.
- We'll annotate the CSV with MAP milestone labels per Emma's request.

Next: confirm 05/07 10:00 for the acceptance run where we'll enable autoscaler and validate fallbacks.

-Marcus

[Attachment: pacing-results-20260507.csv (CSV)]

From: Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>
To: Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Cc: platform@sage-analytics.com
Date: 2026-05-07T16:42:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: Ramp & Pacing Playbook for Final Performance Eval — acceptance confirmation

Team — thanks for the quick turnaround. We've reviewed the annotated CSV and the conservative rate-limit run. The acceptance criteria are satisfied for the steady-state windows and the mitigations for the micro-burst head-of-line issue look reasonable. Sage will sign the MAP contingent on the following:

1) Redwood to provide final ramp_script.yaml and the two token-bucket param sets in a single doc.
2) A post-run summary with the final per-route p95/p99 TTFT and a short note on which workers were pinned and why.
3) A confirmation that autoscaler behavior (enabled in the 05/07 run) did not invalidate the steady-state gating.

If you can provide those by EOD 05/10 we'll finish procurement paperwork and target a production enablement on 06/01.

Emma

Emma Price
Director, Platform Engineering | Sage Analytics
[Attachment: procurement-req.docx (DOCX)]

From: Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.com>
To: Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>, Ava Chen <ava@redwood.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>, Neha Kapoor <neha@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-06T10:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: final perf eval — clarifying pacing expectations

Emma/Team — following the dry-run we've uploaded preliminary results to the shared drive and a compact CSV (pacing-results-20260507.csv). Observations:

- Dry-run spike series produced transient p99 TTFT ~740ms with a single 8-minute window where queue depth trended up; root cause was ingress burst smoothing not applied on their generator.
- Steady-state holds (2x and 4x) met the steady gating: p95 TTFT 120–180ms, p99 320–420ms, observed token/sec within +/-10% of target.
- Micro-bursts exposed a head-of-line effect for 1% of requests tied to an older kernel path (we flagged and pinned those workers in the run).

Actions:
- We'll apply the conservative token-bucket (params in the attached) and re-run the spike suite tomorrow 10:00 PT.
- We'll annotate the CSV with MAP milestone labels per Emma's request.

Next: confirm 05/07 10:00 for the acceptance run where we'll enable autoscaler and validate fallbacks.

-Marcus

[Attachment: pacing-results-20260507.csv (CSV)]

From: Emma Price <emma@sage-analytics.com>

Cc: platform@sage-analytics.com
Date: 2026-05-07T16:42:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: Ramp & Pacing Playbook for Final Performance Eval — acceptance confirmation

Team — thanks for the quick turnaround. We've reviewed the annotated CSV and the conservative rate-limit run. The acceptance criteria are satisfied for the steady-state windows and the mitigations for the micro-burst head-of-line issue look reasonable. Sage will sign the MAP contingent on the following:

1) Redwood to provide final ramp_script.yaml and the two token-bucket param sets in a single doc.
2) A post-run summary with the final per-route p95/p99 TTFT and a short note on which workers were pinned and why.
3) A confirmation that autoscaler behavior (enabled in the 05/07 run) did not invalidate the steady-state gating.

If you can provide those by EOD 05/10 we'll finish procurement paperwork and target a production enablement on 06/01.

Emma

Can your team propose explicit redline language and a short billing mapping (invoice-level example) we can use in procurement? Cc: procurement@orionhs.com
Date: Sun, 3 May 2026 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Ancillary fee waiver trigger — need clarification and redline next steps

Marta —

Appreciate the clear ask and the scenarios. We can do draft redlines and an invoice-level reconciliation table. Quick clarifications so we scope the work correctly:

- When you say \"autoscale credits\" do you mean the reserved-pool burst allowance (Dedicated burst) or on-demand autoscale (hosted)? If both, we will make the redline explicitly list \"reserved burst credits\" and \"on-demand autoscale credits.\"
- For mid-term repricing: do you want the repricing to be treated as a pro-rata true-up on the affected invoice(s) or as a forward-looking adjustment applied to subsequent invoices? I can draft both and label them \"Option A/B\" in the worksheet.

Plan: we will prepare Exhibit B redline + a one-page reconciliation table with two worked examples (pro-rata true-up vs forward-facing adjustment). Target for draft: Thursday EOD PDT. I will loop in Laura (Revenue Ops) and Karthik (Sales Legal) to validate invoice mapping and legal phrasing.

Meeting? If helpful I can schedule a 30-min touch on Thursday morning to walk through the worksheet.

Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Account Exec — Enterprise
Redwood Inference

---



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 4 May 2026 11:20:00 -07:00
Subject: Fwd: Ancillary fee waiver trigger — ask from Orion (RDW-2741)

Laura / Karthik — forwarding Orion's note (below). They want: (i) a clarified waiver trigger that explicitly enumerates credit types; (ii) a worked example for mid-term repricing; (iii) a one‑page invoice mapping for Finance.

Summary of what I propose (drafted below for speed):

Proposed redline snippet for Exhibit B (language we can insert as new paragraph under \"Fees & Credits\"):
\"
Ancillary Fee Waiver: Customer shall be eligible for an Ancillary Fee Waiver if, during any Billing Period, the aggregate credited token units from Reserved Burst Credits and On-Demand Autoscale Credits exceed 20% of Customer's billed token units for such Billing Period. For avoidance of doubt, credits applied solely as promotional or trial credits are excluded from this calculation. When the Ancillary Fee Waiver applies, any Ancillary Fees assessed in that Billing Period shall be reduced pro rata by the percentage necessary to offset the credited units above the 20% threshold, with the resulting waiver reflected as a line-item adjustment on the Billing Period invoice.
\"

Questions for Legal (Karthik):
- Is the \"20%\" threshold defensible? Orion asked for a guardrail but didn't provide target thresholds — do we want to counter with 25% or keep 20% as a concessional anchor?
- The phrase \"for avoidance of doubt\" is helpful but legal prefers examples. Should we include a short worked numeric example inside the clause or keep examples in the reconciliation table only?

Revenue Ops (Laura):
- Can you sketch the invoice mapping: sample invoice lines we should use (token usage, credits applied by type, ancillary fees, waiver adjustment)? I will turn that into the one‑page reconciliation table.

I plan to deliver a complete package (Exhibit B redline docx + reconciliation XLSX + two one‑paragraph options for mid-term repricing) by Thu EOD. If either of you see material blockers please flag.

Date: Fri, 7 May 2026 16:23:00 -07:00
Subject: RE: Ancillary fee waiver trigger — proposed redline + invoice mapping (RDW-2741)

Marta —

Thanks for the patience. Attached are: (1) Exhibit-B_Pricing_Redline_v3.docx (tracked changes) and (2) ancillary-fee-scenarios-ORN-2026.xlsx which includes the one-page reconciliation table plus two worked examples. Summary of the key proposals below so your execs can review quickly.

Executive summary:
- Waiver trigger: we propose enumerating \"Reserved Burst Credits\" and \"On-Demand Autoscale Credits\" explicitly in the clause and using a 25% threshold (we recommend 25% to limit operational edge cases; see rationale below).
- Adjustment approach: by default we propose a pro-rata true-up applied to the invoice for the Billing Period in which the credit usage occurred. Option B (forward-facing adjustment) is provided as an alternative in the worksheet.
- Invoice mapping: sample line-items included — (A) Token Usage (gross), (B) Credits Applied (broken out by type), (C) Ancillary Fees (pre-adjustment), (D) Waiver Adjustment (negative line), (E) Net Amount Due. The reconciliation worksheet shows how to compute (D) programmatically.

Rationale for 25% threshold: we believe 20% is overly permissive and could create continuous waiver cycles on customers with heavy autoscale patterns; 25% balances protection for Orion while preserving predictable revenue. If Orion prefers 20% we can accept it as a one-time concession for the initial 12 months.

Next steps / asks from Orion:
1) Please confirm whether you prefer the 25% threshold or want to keep 20% as requested.
2) Review the redline in Exhibit-B_Pricing_Redline_v3.docx and share any targeted comments (we can turn around minor edits within 24 hours).
3) If you approve the proposed language, I will prepare a clean DocuSign packet and route to your signatory (I have included a DocuSign preview link below).

Proposed meeting: Thu 5/13 09:30-10:00 PT to walk the worksheet and finalize the threshold. If that works for you I will calendar invite and include Laura/Karthik.

Links / artifacts:
- DocuSign preview: https://app.docusign.com/documents/ABCDEFG-ORN-2026
- Ticket for internal ops: JIRA-RDW-5123

Regards,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Exec — Enterprise
Redwood Inference

- Architecture & integration deep-dive (60m): Rishi Malhotra
- Inference cost & optimization (45m): Dev Patel

Other items: AV needs (projector + AirPlay/Mac adapters), whiteboard + sticky notes, and a sign-in sheet. I'm also attaching a travel_form.xlsx for people to fill passenger info / frequent flyer if needed.

Action items:
- Everyone: confirm presenter ownership and list slides you plan to bring.
- Priyom: can you start a shared slide repo and confirm speaker bios?
- Dev: flag any content that needs a live demo and whether we should bring a laptop or use the client's network.

Thanks — coming together tight but should be straightforward once owners lock in.

Best,
Ava Chen
APM, Customer Field Programs
Redwood Inference

Thanks Ava — looks good. Quick updates inline:

- Exec kickoff (30m): I'll own the deck. I'll pull together a 3-slide executive summary (OKRs, risks, ask) and would like a one-page ROI snapshot from Dev showing current cost per inference and projected savings with our recommended changes.
- Architecture session: Rishi, can you also include a short migration plan for their staging environment? 45 min may be tight; suggest 60 min.
- Integration breakout: I propose we split the 90-min block into 60 min workshop + 30 min Q&A; Priyom will coordinate the workshop exercises.

Please upload pre-reads to the Drive folder by May 10. Priyom — can you compile presenter bios (short, 2-3 lines) for the agenda? We should confirm AV: we'll need HDMI + two Mac adapters, one secondary mic, and a conference phone. Also collect dietary restrictions for travel meals.

On travel: prefer to travel the night before; if flights arrive after 8pm, we'll reimburse Uber to the hotel. I'll coordinate with legal on any last-minute expense approvals if needed.

Thanks — looping in the items I mentioned.

—Lauren
Director, Customer Solutions
Redwood Inference
