title: Consolidated security add-on offer — volume credit vs implementation rebate
preview:
From: Sara Lim <sara.lim@pinebrookhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@pinebrookhealth.com
Date: 2026-05-03T16:12:00Z
Subject: Re: Security add-ons question for RFP

Hi Ben —

Thanks for the quick overview earlier. Attaching our RFP (short) and the list of expected volumes for the next 12 months. We need a clearer picture on two things before procurement signs: 1) how you price audit-log retention tiers (30/90/365 days) and 2) whether KMS/HSM is a pass-through or bundled line item with a credit for committed spend.

Two constraints from our side: we need HIPAA-covered data residency in us-east and an option to limit audit-log retention for PII-minimal workflows. If we commit to a 12-month usage baseline (~$650k/year token-equivalent), what are reasonable concession structures you can offer (upfront credits, monthly rebates, reduced implementation fee)?

Appreciate a short proposal we can show Procurement next week.

Attachments: pinebrook_RFP.pdf (PDF, 620KB)

Best,
Sara Lim
Director, Data Platform
Pinebrook Health

---


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Sara Lim <sara.lim@pinebrookhealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-03T18:05:00Z
Subject: Re: Security add-ons question for RFP

Sara —

Thanks for the RFP + volumes. Short answer up front: we can structure a blended approach where audit-log retention is tiered (30/90/365) with per-GB charges and a volume-backed credit, and KMS/HSM can be offered as either a line-item seat/license or included in a bundle with an early-adopter rebate. The tradeoff is between lower recurring unit cost (if you take the bundle) and a lower upfront implementation fee (if you take seats/pass-through).

I've attached a concise pricebook with three starter options and a sketch of concession mixes: 1) \"Pilot Uplift\" (low up-front, small monthly rebate), 2) \"Committed Blend\" (12-month commit, larger annual credit), 3) \"Enterprise Bundle\" (flat recurring bundle, lower per-token unit). See compliance_bundle_pricebook_v3.xlsx.

If helpful I can lay out a numeric example using your $650k baseline and two sample retention mixes. Looping in Laura (billing) and Mina (implementation) to comment on invoice cadence and one-time fee offsets.

Next step: Laura — can you confirm whether we can do a 60% credit on implementation (amortized over 6 months) against a 12-month commit at the $650k baseline? Mina — any quick notes on implementation scoping that would materially affect the credit sizing?

Thanks,

Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com | +1 415-555-0183

Attachments: compliance_bundle_pricebook_v3.xlsx (XLSX, 180KB)

---


From: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-04T14:20:00Z
Subject: Re: Security add-ons question for RFP

Ben / Mina —

Quick confirmation from billing side: we can support an implementation credit up to 60% of the one-time onboarding fee, amortized over the first 6 invoices, conditioned on a signed 12-month committed spend at or above the baseline you supplied. The credit mechanics we'd apply in the SOW/invoice are: one-time credit line applied after first invoice; remaining credit applied monthly as negative invoice line until exhausted.

Couple of clarifying items Procurement will ask:
- We need to define the exact metric used to measure the $650k baseline (list price tokens, discounted tokens, or effective spend after credits). We prefer to anchor to net billed spend.
- For KMS/HSM we can offer two pricing paths: (A) HSM-seat (annual license) + per-event fee, or (B) managed KMS included in bundle with a 20% bundled discount at baseline.

Mina — from an implementation scoping standpoint, audit-log export and residency setup for us-east is ~4-6 weeks if we reuse our standard HIPAA template. If Pinebrook wants a dedicated HSM slot, add 2–3 weeks and an additional minimal setup fee. Depending on how much of the audit-log retention they want to offload (longer than 90 days), we may recommend tiered cold storage which increases recurring cost but reduces infra strain.

I can draft the invoice treatment language and a sample SOW paragraph for the commitment + credit mechanics once Ben confirms he wants us to propose the 60% implementation credit route.

Regards,
Laura Bennett
Head of Billing & Commercial Operations | Redwood Inference

---


From: Sara Lim <sara.lim@pinebrookhealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@pinebrookhealth.com
Date: 2026-05-05T11:47:00Z
Subject: Re: Security add-ons question for RFP

Ben / team —

Thanks for the clear breakdown. Procurement ran the numbers and here is where they landed: they're happy with the 12-month commit and the amortized credit approach, but they want a lower implementation fee (we're proposing you reduce the upfront onboarding fee by 40% rather than just crediting it) plus a 90-day audit-log retention included in the baseline at no extra charge for the first 6 months. In exchange, Pinebrook will sign a 12-month commitment at the $650k baseline and agree to a one-time pilot extension clause for an additional 3 months at the same rates if adoption meets the thresholds.

If you can accept the 40% onboarding reduction (instead of a 60% credit amortized) plus the audit-log retention concession, procurement will push this to legal this week. They also asked for HSM-seat pricing (annual) as a separate line in the quote so they can compare.

Attached: pinebrook_counter_terms.docx (docx)

Best,
Sara

---


From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Sara Lim <sara.lim@pinebrookhealth.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Mina Haddad <mina@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-06T15:22:00Z
Subject: Re: Security add-ons question for RFP

Sara —

Appreciate the clear counter. We can accept Pinebrook's request with the following agreed points:
1) Implementation concession: reduce onboarding invoice by 40% (applied upfront) in lieu of the 60% amortized credit.
2) Audit-log retention: include 90-day retention for audit logs at no incremental charge for the first 6 months; beyond month 6 standard per-GB retention pricing will apply.
3) HSM-seat: we'll include an explicit annual HSM-seat line in the quote (see attached pricebook table), separate from the bundled option.

Laura will produce the updated quote and SOW language today; Mina will scope the timeline and confirm the go-live window (targeting 5–7 weeks from countersign). I'll also generate a DocuSign envelope for the initial order form and routing it to procurement and legal.

If that's all good, I'll mark this as \"approved for legal review\" and send the DocuSign link by end of day.

Ben Carter
Senior Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben@redwood.com

Attachments: compliance_bundle_pricebook_v3.xlsx (updated table), DocuSign: https://app.docusign.com/prepare?doc=PB-2026-0541

title: DR Control Objectives & Failover Constraints — follow-up
preview:
From: Michael Ruiz <michael.ruiz@alcyon.com>
To: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
Cc: Alcyon Security Team <security@alcyon.com>
Date: Sun, 3 May 2026 09:12:00 -07:00
Subject: DR control objectives and operational constraints

Hi Chloe,

Thanks for agreeing to take our DR / continuity questionnaire. We're evaluating Redwood as part of a multi-vendor procurement and need to validate concrete commitments and architectural constraints for our vendor risk team. A few things we need answered so we can advance to the technical review: 

- Confirm demonstrable RTO and RPO targets for the hosted API (per region) and for Dedicated customers.
- Describe what triggers an automated cross-region failover versus a manual switchover.
- Explain any data replication lag windows we should assume for async replication and how that affects session affinity and KV cache state during failover.
- List any operational limitations (e.g., maximum concurrent failovers, maintenance windows that block failover) and how Redwood sequences traffic during a regional outage.

Can you provide an architecture-level summary (diagrams optional) and any runbook extracts, plus a short list of artifacts we can present to our risk committee? Ideally we'd have this by Wed EOD so we can include it in our scheduled review.

Best,
Michael
Michael Ruiz
Senior Infrastructure Risk Manager
Alcyon Financial
[attachment: alcyon_dr_q1.docx (docx, 44KB)]

From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
To: Michael Ruiz <michael.ruiz@alcyon.com>
Date: Sun, 3 May 2026 12:34:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: DR control objectives and operational constraints

Michael —

Appreciate the clear list. I've pulled together an initial summary and the RTO/RPO matrix for each offering (hosted, dedicated, private) — see attached: redwood_dr_summary.pdf and rto_rpo_matrix.csv. High level answers below; Ben and Priya are looped for service-level and contract-level confirmations.

Quick answers: 
- RTO/RPO: Hosted API: typical RTO <= 3 minutes for control-plane re-route, token-generation sessions may have higher tail latency while KV caches rehydrate (detailed per-model numbers in matrix). Dedicated: RTO <= 5 minutes with configured warm capacity; RPO for both is best-effort near-zero for control-plane metadata, up to 60s for live KV caches in async replication modes. Private (VPC/on-prem) depends on customer replication configuration — we provide replication tooling and recommended patterns.

- Failover triggers: Smart routing policy triggers automatic routing to healthy region when regional health-checks cross configured thresholds (loss of 50% capacity or sustained p95 latency SLA breach for >2 minutes). Customers may opt into automatic failover; otherwise we execute a manual-canary switchover per the agreed runbook.

- Replication and cache state: Persistent model artifacts and control-plane metadata are synchronously replicated across control-plane nodes. KV cache is asynchronously replicated — we recommend design patterns that treat KV as cache (replayable or rebuildable). Expect transient session affinity loss during hot failover; we document rehydration steps and provide per-route sticky routing as an interim mitigation.

- Operational limits: Concurrent cross-region failovers are limited by regional capacity reservations and autoscale policies; by default we allow 2 concurrent DR activations for a tenant with automatic fallback to degraded-service mode if capacity is constrained. Maintenance windows do not block failovers but customers can specify blackout windows.

I'll send a slightly expanded runbook extract in the next message (Ben to confirm the contract SLAs).

Regards,
Chloe Martin
Senior Solutions Engineer
Redwood Inference
[attachment: redwood_dr_summary.pdf (pdf, 120KB)]
[attachment: rto_rpo_matrix.csv (csv, 7KB)]

From: Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
Cc: Michael Ruiz <michael.ruiz@alcyon.com>
Date: Mon, 4 May 2026 15:09:00 -04:00
Subject: Internal: Alcyon DR specifics & SLA ask — need confirmation

Ben / Priya —

Looping you in on Alcyon's questionnaire. I provided a short summary but they want artifacts for their risk committee and concrete SLA language around RTO/RPO and concurrency limits. Items to confirm: 

1) Hosted API SLA language we can commit to in an SOW for RTO (control-plane re-route) and any explicit RPO bounds for model state/control metadata.
2) Dedicated tenant: confirm that with reserved warm capacity we can guarantee RTO <= 5 minutes and outline what happens if the warm pool is saturated.
3) The standard runbook extract to give them — we should redact any internal-only operational notes.

Target is to consolidate and send a final package to Michael by Wed 5/6 EOD.

Thanks —
Chloe

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Michael Ruiz <michael.ruiz@alcyon.com>, Chloe Martin <chloe.martin@redwood.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 5 May 2026 21:03:00 +01:00
Subject: Re: DR Control Objectives & Failover Constraints — follow-up

Hi Michael — Chloe, thanks for the summary. Apologies for the delayed follow-up; I wanted to confirm language we can sign to. Below is a concise package you can present to your risk committee.

Executive commitments (can be put into SOW / Annex):
- Hosted API (Standard): Control-plane failover re-route RTO (time to route traffic to healthy region): 3 minutes (p95).
- Hosted API (Standard): Service-level RPO for control-plane metadata: near-zero (synchronous replication). For in-flight KV cache state RPO is not guaranteed — operational guidance: design for cache rehydration; typical cache window for multi-region active/passive is 30–120s depending on model token throughput.
- Dedicated (Reserved Warm Pool): RTO guarantee 5 minutes (p95) when warm-pool reserved at contractual capacity. If warm pool capacity is exceeded, failover will be sequenced with traffic shaping and we provide crediting for impact periods per the SLA.

Failover mechanics:
- Automatic routing: Enabled via Smart Routing policy. Condition thresholds we use: region health < 50% available capacity OR p95 latency > SLA for >120s. Customers can opt-out.
- Manual canary: For customers who prefer manual escalation we provide a documented canary procedure with rollback gates; expected time-to-route for manual canary: 6–20 minutes (depends on traffic volume and customer route configuration).

Data consistency & KV cache note:
- Persistent artifacts and model binaries are stored in replicated object stores with cross-region replication (synchronous for control-plane metadata, async for large artifacts with consistency checks).
- KV cache is a best-effort, asynchronously replicated cache. We provide tooling to export/restore critical session state to a persistent store prior to maintenance windows.

Operational constraints & sequencing:
- Default concurrency: Two simultaneous tenant-level cross-region activations. Requests beyond this will be queued with priority scheduling; degrade-to-read-only is an available fallback.
- Maintenance windows: Customers may define blackout periods where scheduled maintenance avoids automated failover. Emergency failovers still proceed per policy.

Artifacts attached: redwood_dr_summary.pdf (technical summary), rto_rpo_matrix.csv (per-offering numbers), runbook-extract.md (sanitized excerpt suitable for external distribution). I've redacted internal-only runbook steps.

Next steps: if this language works we can produce a short SOW annex and a signed statement of capability. Priya — can you confirm the crediting language and contract clause references (I think it's Annex B-DR-1)?

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Director, Reliability & Field Engineering
Redwood Inference
[attachment: runbook-extract.md (text, 18KB)]

title: POC: Prompt set & evaluation plan — Acme Analytics
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Lara Ortiz <lara.ortiz@acmeanalytics.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>
Date: Fri, 1 May 2026 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: POC: Prompt set & evaluation plan — Acme Analytics

Hi Lara / Ethan —

Great to meet you on the call today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we have shared expectations for the POC/trial: 

Call highlights
- Goal: Validate a Redwood-powered inference pipeline for Acme’s customer support summarization and reranking flows.
- Target workload: low-latency summaries at p95 < 500ms; rerank throughput ~100 qps for peak windows.
- Data: You indicated willingness to share anonymized historical prompts and anonymized interaction logs (~2k rows) for evaluation.

Proposed evaluation plan (high level)
1) Prompt & logs collection (you): 400–600 sample prompts + anonymized response logs covering typical edge cases.
2) Ingest & anonymize (Redwood + Acme): confirm PII removal rules, produce test dataset.
3) Eval run (Redwood): run coverage & quality evals, compute relevance/precision metrics, latency profile.
4) Joint review: present results, identify tuning steps, agree pass/fail criteria.

Suggested success criteria (we can iterate)
- Functional: mean relevance score >= 0.75 on seeded eval set; top-1 accuracy >= 80% on intent classification/rerank tasks.
- Performance: p95 latency < 500ms, median < 120ms for average sequences.
- Safety/compliance: zero instances of PHI leakage in sampled outputs; all logs handled per your retention policy.

Timeline (suggested)
- May 1–7: Acme provides prompt set + anonymized logs; sign any outstanding data transfer addenda.
- May 8–12: Redwood ingests, runs eval harness, and produces initial results.
- May 13: Joint review and next-step decision.

Attachments: POC_Scope_Template.xlsx (template for deliverables and owners)

Can you confirm: 1) who will own extraction of the prompt set on your side, and 2) whether Acme needs Redwood to run PII redaction or you will deliver pre-redacted data?

Thanks — copying Marissa and Vivek for technical and security alignment.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai


From: Lara Ortiz <lara.ortiz@acmeanalytics.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>, procurement@acmeanalytics.com
Date: Mon, 4 May 2026 09:04:00 -0700
Subject: Re: POC: Prompt set & evaluation plan — Acme Analytics

Hi Ben —

Thanks for the clear summary. We’re aligned on the goals. Quick answers:
- Owner for prompt/log extraction: Ethan Moore (cc’d) will lead extraction from our analytics DB and produce the stimulus set.
- Data redaction: we can produce a redacted export if you send a spec for what to strip (email addresses, account ids, free-text PII). Alternatively, we can provide a pseudonymized export and let Redwood do a second-pass redaction. Which do you prefer?

Attached: Acme_prompt_samples.jsonl (small sample, 600 rows) and anonymized_logs.tar.gz (contains session traces). These are initial samples — we will confirm the full export by EOD May 7.

Acme success criteria (internal view)
- Summarization quality: average ROUGE-L >= baseline + 8 points for first-run tuning.
- Intent accuracy: >= 80% as you suggested.
- No downgrade in latency by more than 30% vs current internal baseline.

We also have procurement and legal monitoring the TOS/data transfer side; I’ve looped them in.

—Lara
Lara Ortiz
Product Lead, Customer Experience | Acme Analytics
lara.ortiz@acmeanalytics.com


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
To: Lara Ortiz <lara.ortiz@acmeanalytics.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>, Vivek Kulkarni <vivek.kulkarni@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 5 May 2026 08:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: POC: Prompt set & evaluation plan — Acme Analytics

Thanks for the samples, Lara — they look useful. Technical notes and a proposed lightweight eval harness: 

Eval harness (what Redwood will run)
- Ingest sample JSONL, verify schema, and run anonymization sanity checks.
- Generate gold pairs where available (prompt -> desired summary/rank).
- Run 3 model variants (base open model, quantized variant, and tuned candidate) across the dataset.
- Metrics: relevance (embedding cosine + human-labeled subset), latency histogram, token counts, error/failure rates.

Data sizing guidance
- For a meaningful run: 400–800 prompts across the most common 12 intents, plus 200–500 edge-case prompts.
- If you can provide session context (previous 2–3 messages) that will improve summary quality measurement.

Security/PII
- If you deliver pre-redacted exports, please follow the attached guideline (eval-harness-guidelines.md). If you prefer Redwood to perform redaction, we need an allowlist/blocklist and a sample of 25 records for verification.

Proposed timeline (concrete)
- May 7 (EOD): Acme delivers full export and any data-transfer paperwork.
- May 8–10: Redwood ingests, anonymizes, and runs initial evals.
- May 11: Redwood delivers a results deck and suggested tuning parameters.
- May 13: 45-minute joint review with outcomes and recommended pass/fail.

Next steps (owners)
- Acme (Ethan): full export by 2026-05-07.
- Redwood (Marissa): ingest + initial evals by 2026-05-10.
- Legal/Procurement: confirm DTA by 2026-05-06.

Attachment: eval-harness-guidelines.md

I’ll start the eval run as soon as we receive the full export. Happy to adjust windows if procurement needs more time.

—Marissa
Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference

Subject: POC: Prompt set & evaluation plan — Acme Analytics

Hi Lara / Ethan —

Great to meet you on the call today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we have shared expectations for the POC/trial: 

Call highlights
- Goal: Validate a Redwood-powered inference pipeline for Acme’s customer support summarization and reranking flows.
- Target workload: low-latency summaries at p95 < 500ms; rerank throughput ~100 qps for peak windows.
- Data: You indicated willingness to share anonymized historical prompts and anonymized interaction logs (~2k rows) for evaluation.

Proposed evaluation plan (high level)
1) Prompt & logs collection (you): 400–600 sample prompts + anonymized response logs covering typical edge cases.
2) Ingest & anonymize (Redwood + Acme): confirm PII removal rules, produce test dataset.
3) Eval run (Redwood): run coverage & quality evals, compute relevance/precision metrics, latency profile.
4) Joint review: present results, identify tuning steps, agree pass/fail criteria.

Suggested success criteria (we can iterate)
- Functional: mean relevance score >= 0.75 on seeded eval set; top-1 accuracy >= 80% on intent classification/rerank tasks.
- Performance: p95 latency < 500ms, median < 120ms for average sequences.
- Safety/compliance: zero instances of PHI leakage in sampled outputs; all logs handled per your retention policy.

Timeline (suggested)
- May 1–7: Acme provides prompt set + anonymized logs; sign any outstanding data transfer addenda.
- May 8–12: Redwood ingests, runs eval harness, and produces initial results.
- May 13: Joint review and next-step decision.

Attachments: POC_Scope_Template.xlsx (template for deliverables and owners)

Can you confirm: 1) who will own extraction of the prompt set on your side, and 2) whether Acme needs Redwood to run PII redaction or you will deliver pre-redacted data?

Thanks — copying Marissa and Vivek for technical and security alignment.

Best,
Ben Carter

title: MAP: ChatStream concurrency sweep + TTFT / streaming latencies
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-03T16:12:00-07:00
Subject: MAP: ChatStream concurrency sweep + TTFT / streaming latencies
To: Emma Li <emma.li@acme-labs.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.com>

Hi Emma —

As promised in the MAP, attached are the results from our ChatStream concurrency sweep run yesterday and a short plan for acceptance criteria and next steps. Key context up front: we ran on the Redwood-verified 13B INT8 variant (redwood-13B-int8-v1) with streaming enabled, prompt length distribution centered at 64 tokens and max generation 256 tokens. The workload simulates chat bursts (many short messages) and long-turn generations to exercise both TTFT and streaming per-token latency.

Summary of top-line numbers (see attachments for full CSV + plots):
- TTFT (time-to-first-token) p50/p95/p99 by concurrency (clients):
  - concurrency 1 => p50 42ms, p95 88ms, p99 140ms
  - concurrency 2 => p50 44ms, p95 95ms, p99 170ms
  - concurrency 4 => p50 48ms, p95 120ms, p99 240ms
  - concurrency 8 => p50 56ms, p95 180ms, p99 420ms
  - concurrency 16 => p50 90ms, p95 380ms, p99 1.1s

- Streaming per-token output latency (time between token emission events, measured after first token): p50 9ms, p95 35ms, p99 85ms (stable across concurrencies up to 8; degrades beyond where batching kicks in).

- Aggregate throughput (tokens/sec observed across test harness): 1 => 450 t/s, 4 => 1.6k t/s, 8 => 2.7k t/s, 16 => 3.0k t/s (saturates due to kernel scheduling and increased contention).

Short interpretation: single-client TTFT and streaming latency are well within interactive bounds. At higher concurrency the TTFT p95 climbs due to queueing and batching (our runtime favors throughput when concurrency increases). The streaming per-token latency remains acceptable through 8 concurrent chat streams, which matches Acme's target concurrency in the MAP.

Proposed acceptance criteria for the MAP (we recommend these be formalized in the MAP deliverables):
- TTFT p95 <= 200ms at target concurrency (8 concurrent chat sessions)
- Streaming per-token p95 <= 40ms (for short-turn chat UX)
- End-to-end 95th latency for 256-token responses <= 2.5s

Immediate asks / next steps:
1) Can Acme confirm target concurrency and acceptable p95 thresholds? (From MAP: target was 6–10 concurrent streams; please confirm exact SLA.)
2) If agreed, we'll lock these acceptance criteria into the MAP and run a prioritized re-test with environment parity to Acme's staging network (we'll coordinate with Jorge from SRE partner).
3) We propose a final MAP checkpoint on 2026-05-13 to sign-off performance and push to the closure milestone on 2026-05-15.

Attachments: chatstream-concurrency-sweep-20260503.csv (CSV), ttft-summary-figures-20260503.pdf (PDF)

Thanks —
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Emma Li <emma.li@acme-labs.com>
Date: 2026-05-03T19:05:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: ChatStream concurrency sweep + TTFT / streaming latencies
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Jorge Alvarez <jorge.alvarez@srepartner.io>, Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>

Ben —

Thanks for the quick turnaround and the attachments. High level this looks promising. A couple quick clarifications before we lock acceptance criteria: 
- Our MAP targets 8 concurrent active users per model replica (peak). Can you confirm that your concurrency numbers are per simulated client (i.e., 8 concurrent sessions hitting one replica) rather than aggregated across multiple replicas?
- For streaming per-token p95 you reported 35ms — that's great. Can you break that down by sequence position (first 16 tokens vs. tokens 17–128) to ensure there's no warm-up effect?

We'll loop in our SRE (Jorge) on environment parity. For the timeline, 2026-05-13 checkpoint and 2026-05-15 close look fine to us — provided the follow-up run is in our staging VPC.

Emma Li
Head of ML Infrastructure, Acme Labs


From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-03T20:30:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: ChatStream concurrency sweep + TTFT / streaming latencies
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.com>
Cc: Emma Li <emma.li@acme-labs.com>, Jorge Alvarez <jorge.alvarez@srepartner.io>

Emma / team —

Short answers:
- Concurrency: the numbers in Ben's CSV are per single replica (we forced traffic to one serving instance). We'll re-run an identical harness pointed at Acme's staging VPC and confirm replica-level numbers.
- Streaming breakdown: initial analysis attached to the PDF, but quick point: first 16 tokens show slightly higher per-token latency (p95 ~ 42ms) due to initial KV cache and kernel startup; tokens 17–128 are ~30ms p95. We'll include a position-bucketed breakdown in the re-run export.

I'll coordinate the scheduled staging run. Vanessa, can you own the on-call hook with Jorge for the 5/10–5/12 window? I will prepare the re-run script and baseline configs (model version, seq len, sampling).

Marissa Cole
Product Lead, Redwood Console


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: 2026-05-04T23:45:00-07:00
Subject: Re: MAP: ChatStream concurrency sweep + TTFT / streaming latencies
To: Emma Li <emma.li@acme-labs.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa_ortiz@redwood.com>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwood.com>, Jorge Alvarez <jorge.alvarez@srepartner.io>

Emma / Jorge / team —

Thanks for the quick reviews and confirmation. Per Marissa's note, we re-ran the job in Redwood's staging with a position-bucketed export and pushed the harness into Acme's staging VPC for one hour on 2026-05-04 to capture network effects. Updated highlights (new CSV attached to Drive link above):
- Replica-level TTFT p95 at 8 concurrent sessions (in Acme staging VPC): 172ms (meets proposed acceptance p95 <= 200ms)
- TTFT p99 at 8 concurrent sessions: 395ms (documented in the MAP as an informational metric)
- Streaming per-token p95 (tokens 17–128): 33ms; p95(first 16 tokens): 48ms
- End-to-end 95th latency for 256-token responses: 2.1s (within the 2.5s target)

We recommend the following MAP updates before the 5/13 checkpoint:
- Lock acceptance criteria: TTFT p95 <= 200ms @ 8 concurrent replica-level sessions; streaming per-token p95 <= 40ms for stable-turns; E2E 95th <= 2.5s
- Add a regression alert in the MAP tied to the shared prompt set (if p95 TTFT increases > 25% from baseline then trigger a rollback to a lower-batched config).
- Schedule final sign-off on 2026-05-13 and close on 2026-05-15 if no regressions.

Next action items assigned:
- Vanessa: coordinate on-call window with Jorge and validate infra (due 2026-05-10)
- Marissa: upload the detailed position-bucketed CSV and a short runbook for interpreting p95 shifts (due 2026-05-09)
- Emma: confirm final wording for acceptance criteria in the MAP (due 2026-05-10)

I'll update the HubSpot MAP doc and push an internal note to the sales lead. Thanks all — close is looking good.

Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
Attachments: updated CSV uploaded to Drive (link above)

Ben / Amelia —

I've reviewed the ClearMed redline and attached a legal markup (Redline_Legal_Notes_v1.docx). High-level observations:
- Section 2.3 (Data Export Controls): ClearMed wants a blanket prohibition on transfers to countries without an adequacy decision. We cannot accept an absolute prohibition; propose: a Transfer Impact Addendum (TIA) + documented technical/organizational safeguards.
- SCCs: the vendor-processor role mapping needs to be explicit. We should accept SCCs with a short rider that documents processor lists and subprocessor approvals rather than heavy MSA edits.
- Liability caps: No new carveouts should expand exposure; propose a narrow exception only for identified data subject categories (health data) and capped at order form value of the initial year.

Actionable asks for ClearMed legal: please confirm which EU jurisdictions they consider "restricted" and whether they'd accept an HSM residency SLA (we can offer EU-resident HSMs for an additional charge).

Attaching the markup. I'll call out required redline changes inline.

Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference

3) DNS swap to prior inference endpoint (30m TTL assumed)
4) Monitor 15m for success, then re‑open traffic; if failed re‑run provisioning request and escalate to SRE on PagerDuty

Cc: Diego Fernandez <diego.fernandez@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace.oconnor@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 4 May 2026 09:30:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Blank responses from model during nightly batch — closure and recommendations

Hi Sara — thanks for the logs and for replaying. Summary of our findings and next steps:

What we looked at:
- Tenant telemetry for zs-prod-9 across 02:05–02:25 PT.
- Worker logs from rwi-node-47 for the relevant timeframe.
- Model inference traces for the request ids you provided.
- Replays you submitted (both your local replay and our staging replay).

What we observed:
- We found a handful of completed requests that returned HTTP 200 with content-length 0 originating from rwi-node-47. No 5xx, no engine crashes, and no GPU OOM recorded.
- Worker rwi-node-47 showed a transient I/O hiccup on the host at 02:12:13 (brief spike in syscall latency) which coincides with the timestamps you saw. The spike did not trigger container restarts and resolved within ~2s.
- Replays did not reproduce the zero-length body.

Conclusion:
- We cannot conclusively confirm a definitive code bug in the inference runtime. The most plausible explanation is a transient host-level I/O latency spike that resulted in a response being sent before token aggregation completed, leading to an empty payload for a small number of requests. Because the host IO spike was transient and did not cause wider instability, the behavior is non-deterministic and not reproducible by replay.

Actions taken by Redwood:
- Marked rwi-node-47 for deeper postmortem and added it to our priority health-check sweep.
- Added a short-lived mitigation: we’ve increased the worker-side response verification window for your tenant to reduce the chance of early-empty responses while we complete the node investigation. This is a conservative change and will not affect throughput for your current quota.
- Added additional telemetry to capture body-length metrics and syscall latencies for 48h across the fleet and will alert immediately on any recurrence for zs-prod-9.

Recommended actions for ZetaSoft:
- If possible, add a lightweight client-side verification that retries on 0-length bodies (idempotent-only). We can provide a sample snippet.
- If this affects SLAs or downstream validation, let us know — we can temporarily route your traffic away from rwi-node-47 nodes until we complete a postmortem.

Ticket & next steps:
- Incident record: INC-2026-047 (https://app.redwood.ai/incidents/INC-2026-047)
- For now we are classifying this as "unconfirmed / transient host I/O" and closing the immediate customer-facing ticket. We will reopen if telemetry indicates recurrence.
- If you see new occurrences, please reply to this thread with the exact request id and a timestamp and we'll prioritize an immediate trace.

Thanks for the thorough report and logs — sorry for the disruption.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Account Engineer | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai

title: Spike in 5xx/429s — SDK retry amplification (FinVanta)
preview:
From: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 09:10:00 -0700
Subject: Elevated 5xx/429s and timeouts impacting transactions

Hi Evelyn,

We're seeing a high volume of 5xx and 429 responses against Redwood starting ~08:45 PDT this morning which is causing timeouts in our payment processing pipeline and a lot of retries on our side. Latency across affected requests has jumped from ~120ms to >2s depending on burst. Our monitoring shows our client SDK attempted exponential retries and appears to be amplifying load during the spike.

Attached: finvanta_request-traces_20260502.json — it's a small sample of traces and request IDs.

This is impacting customer transactions and the ops team is escalating. Can you advise immediate mitigations we can apply (client/server) while you investigate? We need a quick plan to stop the retry amplification.

Thanks,
Samantha Lee
Platform Reliability Lead
FinVanta

--
FinVanta platform-ops: platform-ops@finvanta.com


From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 09:18:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Elevated 5xx/429s and timeouts impacting transactions

Samantha — thanks for the quick heads-up and for the traces. We're escalating this to our incident response and engineering immediately.

Immediate actions we are taking (next 30–90m):
- Triage: Nikhil is pulling server-side rates and token-bucket metrics and will look for retry amplification patterns.
- Temporary mitigation: we'll propose a short-lived server-side per-account throttling and a 10s circuit-breaker for rapid recurrence if amplification is detected.
- Ask for your help: can you temporarily reduce client-side retries to at most 2 attempts and add jitter (200–800ms) while we validate? That change should reduce amplification fast.

I'll follow up with a concrete runbook and a timeline. Appreciate the attachments — we'll correlate.

Evelyn Hart
Head of Customer Success, Redwood Inference


From: Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwood.ai>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Cc: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 09:36:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Elevated 5xx/429s and timeouts impacting transactions

On Sat, May 2, 2026 at 09:18, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai> wrote:
> Samantha — thanks for the quick heads-up...

Quick analysis from the initial logs I pulled:
- We see a spike in 429s followed by retries from the same client prefixes. Many clients are configured with aggressive default retry policies (exp backoff but very short initial window and no jitter).
- The request distribution shows repeated bursts within <1s windows (retry loops) for the same user sessions — classic retry amplification.
- Server-side, the per-account token bucket for FinVanta exhausted and we started returning 429s; this triggered more retries which produced transient 5xx errors downstream when queues filled.

Recommended immediate technical mitigations (we can implement within 45m):
1) Apply a temporary per-account throttling window and a 10s cooldown if a client exceeds 50% of its steady-state request rate. This will reduce the amplification loop.
2) Enable a short-lived circuit breaker: after N 429s in M seconds, return an informative 429 with Retry-After header of 10s and a structured JSON recommending client backoff.
3) On our SDK side: add a safety cap that prevents >3 retries by default and enables randomized jitter. We can push a conservative hotfix flag for SDKs used by FinVanta (if they're using our SDK).

I'll attach the rate-limiter CSV and a short runbook excerpt.

-Nikhil
Senior Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference

Attachments: rate-limiter-metrics-0502.csv


From: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>
To: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Nikhil Sharma <nikhil.sharma@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 10:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Temporary client-side recommendations and quick patch

Hi Samantha — thanks for sharing the traces. Two immediate, low-risk changes you can apply on your side within minutes:

1) Client SDK config (recommended): set max_attempts=2, base_backoff=250ms, max_backoff=1200ms, enable_jitter=true (range 200–800ms).
2) If you control a small percentage of traffic, enable canary client toggles so the change targets 10% first.

If you confirm the SDK version you are using (send package.json or SDK version string), we can tell you whether the SDK has a built-in safety cap you can flip on.

We will:
- Stage a server-side throttling rule and the circuit breaker within 45m.
- Open a post-incident runbook and propose a follow-up for a longer-term fix (SDK defaults + server-side defense).

Attached: redwood_mitigation_runbook_v1.pdf — contains exact curl examples and a short validation checklist.

Monica Patel
Customer Success Engineer, Redwood Inference


From: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
To: Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>, Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 11:17:00 -07:00
Subject: Exec update (FYI) — FinVanta incident and ask for credit approval

Evelyn/Rachel — brief update for partner leadership:
- Impact: FinVanta production saw ~18% failed transactions during the 08:45–10:30 window, customer reported revenue-impacting timeouts.
- Root cause (initial): retry amplification due to aggressive client-side defaults + temporary exhaustion of per-account rate buckets.
- Immediate fix: coordinated client-side throttling + server-side cooldown/circuit-breaker rolled out by eng.
- Ask: customer is requesting a small one-time service credit while we complete the postmortem and deliver an SDK safety update. Can leadership approve up to a $7,500 credit (commercial team to document)?

I'll hold on external comms until we have the postmortem draft and a remediation timeline.

-Marcus Lin
Account Executive, Redwood Inference


From: Samantha Lee <samantha.lee@finvanta.com>
To: Monica Patel <monica.patel@redwood.ai>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn.hart@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rachel Kim <rachel.kim@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.ai>
Date: Sun, 3 May 2026 15:47:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Spike in 5xx/429s — SDK retry amplification (FinVanta)

Monica/Nikhil/Evelyn — quick update from our side: we toggled the SDK retry cap to 2 attempts and enabled jitter as suggested around 09:35. The error rate dropped substantially within ~10 minutes and transaction throughput recovered. The temporary server-side throttling/circuit-breaker also helped for high-volume endpoints.

We'd like the postmortem and SDK default changes documented and a proposed timeline for safer SDK defaults (preventive). Regarding the credit, we appreciate the offer; please have Marcus send formal terms and we'll review with finance.

Thanks for the rapid response — we'd like to schedule a 30-minute bridge tomorrow to review the root cause and next steps.

Best,
Samantha

--
Forwarded message (sample traces) available upon request.

title: Request: add pass-through egress + SOW split to Order Form
preview:
From: Carlos Ramos <carlos@novatech.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: procurement@novatech.com
Date: Tue, 27 Apr 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Request: Add pass-through egress and SOW split to Order Form

Hi Ben,

Quick note from our infra / procurement sync — we've started to see material third-party egress charges on some of our inference traffic (CloudProvider egress and inter-region replication) that we didn't forecast into the order form rates. Procurement has asked if we can: 1) add a pass-through clause in the Order Form to bill cloud egress as a separate reimbursable item, and 2) split the Statement of Work so the managed operations piece is a separately priced deliverable (we want clear hours/SLAs for ops vs. platform onboarding).

High level asks:
- Add language to Order Form that allows NovaTech to be invoiced for third-party cloud egress costs on a monthly true-up basis, with attachable receipts.
- Add a SOW exhibit that separates Implementation (fixed) from Managed Ops (time & materials or monthly retainer).
- Clarify amortization of the one-time setup fee across the first 12 months (customer prefers this).

Procurement wants a redline and indicative pricing by next Thursday so they can include it in their budget presentation.

Attached: nova_change_request_v1.pdf (pdf) — contains sample egress statements and the SOW outline.

Can you advise next steps and whether Redwood's legal needs anything specific for pass-through wording?

Thanks,
Carlos Ramos
Director, Cloud Ops
NovaTech Systems



From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Carlos Ramos <carlos@novatech.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 27 Apr 2026 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Request: Add pass-through egress and SOW split to Order Form

Carlos — thanks for the clear ask and the attachment. We can accommodate this; below are two practical paths that procurement typically signs quickly. Once you confirm a preferred option I'll push redlines through Legal & Finance.

title: Advance commitment schedule & Net terms negotiation for multi-year deal
preview:
From: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@cloudfin.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: Marco Bianchi <marco.bianchi@cloudfin.com>
Date: Mon, 20 Apr 2026 10:15:00 -0700
Subject: Multi-year commitment — payment schedule and Net terms question

Hi Alex,

Thanks again for the demo last week. We ran the numbers with Finance and Procurement and want to discuss a multi-year advance commitment (3 years) with an annual billing cadence but need flexibility on payment timing. High-level ask:

- Can Redwood accommodate a 3-year, annual prepay with a larger discount if we agree to an initial partial upfront (25% at contract signature) and the remainder billed annually?
- Our procurement policy prefers Net 90 for invoice settlements. Is Net 90 possible for the annual invoices if we do partial upfront?
- Alternatively, would Redwood consider a milestone schedule (25% upfront, 50% at production launch, 25% at year 1 anniversary) for the first year and then annual prepay thereafter?

We can share a PO for the upfront amount as soon as we have a signed SOW/contract. Also attaching our internal purchase guidelines for reference (see attachment). Can you propose a few payment schedule options and the corresponding term discounts?

Appreciate a quick outline so we can get Legal/Finance aligned.

Best,
Elena
Director, Procurement — CloudFin Analytics

Attachment: cloudfin_purchase_policy.pdf (referenced)

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.inference.com>
To: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@cloudfin.com>
Cc: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.inference.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Wed, 22 Apr 2026 09:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Multi-year commitment — payment schedule and Net terms question

Hi Elena — thanks for the clear summary and the policy doc. Quick responses and three proposed options below (we tested economics for each with Finance):

Option A — 3yr annual prepay (single invoice per year)- Discount: 18% on list for 3-year commit- Upfront: 25% at signature (applied as credit to Y1)- Invoicing: annual invoices for the outstanding year amount- Terms: Net 60 on annual invoices (we can extend to Net 75 with exec approval; Net 90 requires a finance exception)

Option B — Milestone-first schedule (hybrid)- Discount: 20% on list (higher due to split risk)- Payment: 25% upfront, 50% at production launch milestone, 25% at Y1 anniversary- Terms: Net 60 for milestone invoices post-signature; remaining future annual payments paid upfront or Net 60 depending on corporate PO

Option C — Heavier upfront with deeper discount- Discount: 22% if CloudFin prepays full first year + 25% upfront at signature for commitment (applies to first invoice only)- Payment: 50% upfront credited toward Y1 and Y2 ramp schedule; remaining annual prepayments due at start of each year- Terms: Net 60 on any subsequent invoice; upfront amount via PO at signature

Notes:
- Net 90 is uncommon for prepay deals at Redwood; we can pursue a Net 90 finance exception if CloudFin can provide a corporate credit evaluation and a PO for the upfront portion.
- Milestone schedules introduce collection risk; Finance prefers a larger upfront or a confirmed PO for the upfront tranche.
- I attached a draft payment schedule spreadsheet (redwood_payment_schedule.xlsx) and a short SOW outline (SOW_v2.pdf) for your review.

If any of these look promising I can loop in Priya to request the formal finance exception and get an initial DS (DocuSign) ready. Which option should I prioritize?

Thanks,
Alex
Account Exec — Redwood Inference

Attachments: redwood_payment_schedule.xlsx, SOW_v2.pdf

From: Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.inference.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.inference.com>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.inference.com>
Date: Thu, 23 Apr 2026 16:45:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Multi-year commitment — payment schedule and Net terms question

Alex — looping in Finance input and formal exception guidance. High level Finance stance: we can entertain exceptions for Net 90 on billed amounts when there is sufficient upfront or an irrevocable PO. Detailed recommendation: 

- Preferred structure (Finance-approved): 25% upfront at signature (PO accepted), 50% at production launch (invoice Net 60), remainder (25%) invoiced at year-end with Net 60. This is close to Option B and balances risk.
- For a deeper discount (20%+), require the customer to prepay at least the first year (annual upfront) or provide a fully executed corporate PO that covers the upfront tranche.
- If CloudFin insists on Net 90 across annual invoices, require a formal credit review and approval by Head of Finance (Rishi). That review typically adds 5–7 business days.

Action items for Alex: please confirm which option Elena prefers and attach (1) proposed PO for upfront tranche or (2) corporate credit references if they need Net 90. If they sign for the upfront PO, we can approve a 20% discount for the milestone hybrid or 22% for heavier upfront.

Priya
Director, Finance Operations — Redwood Inference

Attachment: finance_exception_template.docx (referenced)

From: Elena Rossi <elena.rossi@cloudfin.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.inference.com>
Cc: Marco Bianchi <marco.bianchi@cloudfin.com>
Date: Sat, 2 May 2026 09:05:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Multi-year commitment — payment schedule and Net terms question

Alex / Priya — thanks for the clear options and the spreadsheet. We discussed internally and are inclined to go with the milestone-first schedule you and Finance recommended (Option B variant):

- Agreement: 3-year commitment, 20% discount- Payment: 25% upfront at signature (we will issue PO for this immediately), 50% at production launch, 25% at Y1 anniversary- Terms: Net 60 on milestone invoices; for the remaining annual prepayments after Y1 we will aim for upfront annual payment but may request Net 60 on those invoices

Next steps from our side: Elena to send PO for the 25% upfront today. We will also share any corporate credit references only if Redwood requires Net 90 later. Please send the revised SOW and contract with those payment terms and the DocuSign link and we'll route for signature.

As a note for legal: we expect a standard liability clause and a typical SLA commitment; no unusual redlines anticipated but Legal may request a short review window.

Thanks — looking forward to closing.
Elena
Director, Procurement — CloudFin Analytics

Links included: DocuSign (https://www.docusign.com/link/abc123-redwood-cloudfin)
Attachments referenced by Redwood: SOW_v2.pdf, redwood_payment_schedule.xlsx

title: Model shortlist + token buckets for LedgerPay onboarding
preview:
From: Ellis Turner <ellis@ledgerpay.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>
Cc: platform-eng@ledgerpay.com
Date: Mon, 27 Apr 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick follow on model fit inputs

Hi Ben / Monica,

Thanks for the demo last week — team is excited. I pulled together a quick token profile from our staging logs and a couple of operational constraints:

- Primary flows: invoice auto-reconciliation (short prompts, avg 120 tokens), customer dispute triage (interactive, avg 400 tokens, often includes 2–3 prior messages as context), and batch OCR post-processing (longer prompts for extraction, avg 1,800 tokens occasionally hitting 3k for multi-page).
- Estimated steady-state QPS: 2 r/s across the three flows, with 20% peak windows that could push to 6 r/s for 5–10 minutes.
- Latency targets: <220ms P50 for single-turn reconciliation, <600ms P95 for interactive triage (we can tolerate slightly higher tail for long batch jobs).
- Security: we need SOC2 evidence and confirmation about KV/prefix caching controls (we cannot cache PII).

Attaching the staging token export. Can you propose a shortlist of models and an initial token-bucket sizing approach for onboarding? We're targeting a 6-week pilot starting mid-May.

Ellis
LedgerPay — Platform

Attachment: ledgerpay_token_profile.xlsx (xlsx, 55KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
To: Ellis Turner <ellis@ledgerpay.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 27 Apr 2026 11:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick follow on model fit inputs

Ellis — thanks for the export and the latency targets; super helpful. High level recommendation and clarifying questions below.

1) Shortlist (starter + spares):
   - M1-small-8q (fast 8-bit quantized variant) — candidate for reconciliation (low latency, cheap).
   - M2-compat-16 (full FP16) — fallback for interactive triage when quality matters.
   - LLM-long-32k (sized for OCR and long multi-page context) — reserve for batch OCR jobs.

2) Token-bucket sketch for pilot (per-minute units):
   - Steady-state reserve: 120k tokens/min across pool (covers 2 r/s with headroom).
   - Peak buffer (20% windows): additional 60k tokens/min allocated to a burst bucket for 10 mins.
   - Long-job pool: separate lane with pre-warmed instances for jobs >2k tokens to avoid head-of-line blocking.

Questions to confirm:
- Are the PII fields stripped before sending to Redwood, or do you need an in-flight redaction step?
- Can the batch OCR be scheduled in a 2-4 hour window if needed, or is it real-time?

I've attached a compact model shortlist PDF with perf numbers from our verify runs. Monica/Grace — can you weigh in on caching and the pilot scope? I'll follow up with a rough cost estimate once we lock the lane sizing.

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Customer Success — Redwood Inference

Attachment: model_shortlist_v2.pdf (pdf, 120KB)

From: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ellis Turner <ellis@ledgerpay.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.ai>, Marcus Lin <marcus@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 28 Apr 2026 09:38:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Quick follow on model fit inputs

Thanks Ben — good start. A few operational notes and a proposed pilot split: 

- Caching: we can enable prefix/KV caching for non-PII conversational context. For PII-sensitive fields, we will mark those requests "no-cache" at the route level. That satisfies your SOC2 control requirement (we'll provide the audit config).
- Autoscaling policy: suggest a two-lane policy — low-latency lane (M1-small-8q) with continuous batching knob tightened for sub-300ms P50, and an accuracy lane (M2-compat-16) that uses slightly looser batching to meet quality. Long batch OCR jobs should route to the LLM-long-32k pool which can be pre-warmed at scheduled intervals to reduce cold-starts.
- Observability: we'll add a temporary trace tag for the pilot routes and surface token-level latency/cost in the Console dashboard.

Pilot proposal (6 weeks):
Week 1: Integration & baseline runs — run 48-hour smoke, confirm PII flags, validate end-to-end latency.
Weeks 2–4: Controlled ramp — shift 20/40/60% of production traffic to Redwood lanes, measure quality regressions with the supplied eval set.
Weeks 5–6: Peak soak + runbook handover — exercise 20% peak windows and finalize docs for handover to your SRE.

Operational ask from LedgerPay: please confirm whether OCR batches can be deferred to a 2-hour window for pre-warming optimization; if not, we'll propose a higher sustained allocation for the long-job pool.

Marcus — can you prepare a quick cost delta for tightening batching vs. moving traffic to M2? I'll attach a draft pilot scope doc (pilot_scope.docx) to the HubSpot ticket.

Best,
Monica Patel
Customer Success Manager — Redwood Inference

From: Ellis Turner <ellis@ledgerpay.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>
Cc: platform-eng@ledgerpay.com
Date: Mon, 27 Apr 2026 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick follow on model fit inputs

Hi Ben / Monica,

Thanks for the demo last week — team is excited. I pulled together a quick token profile from our staging logs and a couple of operational constraints:

- Primary flows: invoice auto-reconciliation (short prompts, avg 120 tokens), customer dispute triage (interactive, avg 400 tokens, often includes 2–3 prior messages as context), and batch OCR post-processing (longer prompts for extraction, avg 1,800 tokens occasionally hitting 3k for multi-page).
- Estimated steady-state QPS: 2 r/s across the three flows, with 20% peak windows that could push to 6 r/s for 5–10 minutes.
- Latency targets: <220ms P50 for single-turn reconciliation, <600ms P95 for interactive triage (we can tolerate slightly higher tail for long batch jobs).
- Security: we need SOC2 evidence and confirmation about KV/prefix caching controls (we cannot cache PII).

Attaching the staging token export. Can you propose a shortlist of models and an initial token-bucket sizing approach for onboarding? We're targeting a 6-week pilot starting mid-May.

Ellis
LedgerPay — Platform

Attachment: ledgerpay_token_profile.xlsx (xlsx, 55KB)

From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>

Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.ai>, Grace O'Connor <grace@redwood.ai>

Ellis — thanks for the export and the latency targets; super helpful. High level recommendation and clarifying questions below.

1) Shortlist (starter + spares):
   - M1-small-8q (fast 8-bit quantized variant) — candidate for reconciliation (low latency, cheap).
   - M2-compat-16 (full FP16) — fallback for interactive triage when quality matters.
   - LLM-long-32k (sized for OCR and long multi-page context) — reserve for batch OCR jobs.

2) Token-bucket sketch for pilot (per-minute units):
   - Steady-state reserve: 120k tokens/min across pool (covers 2 r/s with headroom).
   - Peak buffer (20% windows): additional 60k tokens/min allocated to a burst bucket for 10 mins.
   - Long-job pool: separate lane with pre-warmed instances for jobs >2k tokens to avoid head-of-line blocking.

Questions to confirm:
- Are the PII fields stripped before sending to Redwood, or do you need an in-flight redaction step?
- Can the batch OCR be scheduled in a 2-4 hour window if needed, or is it real-time?

I've attached a compact model shortlist PDF with perf numbers from our verify runs. Monica/Grace — can you weigh in on caching and the pilot scope? I'll follow up with a rough cost estimate once we lock the lane sizing.

Cheers,
Ben Carter

title: Reserved GPU pool — token economics & throughput check
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Emma Li <emma@brightbridge.ai>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: Sat, 25 Apr 2026 15:12:00 -0700
Subject: Reserved GPU pool — token economics & throughput check

Hi Emma — great to meet you on the call earlier. Quick recap and a few follow-ups so we can size a Reserved (dedicated) pool that meets BrightBridge's cost and latency targets.

Summary from the call:
- Primary workload: multi-tenant batch scoring + interactive chat for end-clients (mix of short and long sequences).
- Estimated steady-state: ~200M tokens / month.
- Burst profile: occasional peaks up to ~2.5M tokens / day during campaigns.
- Target unit economics: exploring $150–$220 per 1M tokens for the reserved configuration (goal is to be below $200 if possible).
- Latency SLO: median < 250ms for short chat (<=256 tokens); throughput target ~800–1,200 tokens/sec aggregate for production window.

Can you confirm the steady-state and burst numbers above? If BrightBridge expects a materially higher or lower pattern (e.g., sustained 400M+/month), that changes the recommendation. Also please confirm any strict preferencing for GPU type (A100 vs H100) or constraints from your procurement/security teams.

Next items from our side once you confirm:
- We’ll run a quick mapping exercise (conservative and aggressive) that maps expected token volume to GPU counts and expected $/1M token for Reserved pools.
- Include a runway plan for burst capacity (transient burst lanes vs permanent reserve).
- Attach an initial straw-man quote and a token profile workbook for you to edit.

Attachments: redwood_reserved_quote_v1.pdf (straw-man pricing), token_profile_breakdown.xlsx (token mix assumptions)

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (415) 555-0186

----
Note: Ben to BrightBridge post-demo — linking Vanessa and Priyom for sizing and technical follow-through.

From: Emma Li <emma@brightbridge.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 27 Apr 2026 08:07:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Reserved GPU pool — token economics & throughput check

Hi Ben — thanks for the recap and the materials. Confirming the numbers with our ops team; initial answers below: 

Confirmed profile:
- Steady-state: 200M tokens/month (production baseline).
- Campaign bursts: roughly 2–3 days/month of elevated traffic; peak ~2.2–2.8M tokens/day.
- Concurrent interactive sessions: typically 60–120 active sessions during peak windows, median session length ~180 tokens.

Budget:
- We’re internally modeling a $/1M-token target of $150–$200 to hit our unit economics. If we can land at or below $180 that’s ideal.

GPU preference / constraints:
- Procurement prefers A100 family for price predictability, but engineering favors H100 for lower latency on our long-context models.
- No hard compliance blocker for either GPU; purely a trade-off of cost vs tail latency.

Questions for Redwood:
1) For a baseline of 200M/month with the peak profile above, which reserved pool sizes (GPU count & type) do you recommend as 2 options (cost-optimal and latency-optimized)?
2) What’s the estimated $/1M tokens for each option (assume model is a 70B decoder with 512 token KV cache average)?
3) How do you recommend handling campaign bursts — short-lived burst lanes or periodic scale-up of the reserve?

We can share a five-week token log if that helps with finer-grain sizing.

Thanks — Emma
Emma Li
Head of Platform Engineering | BrightBridge AI
emma@brightbridge.ai

----
On Sat, Apr 25, 2026 at 3:12 PM Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com> wrote:
> Hi Emma — great to meet you on the call earlier. Quick recap and a few follow-ups so we can size a Reserved (dedicated) pool that meets BrightBridge's cost and latency targets.



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Emma Li <emma@brightbridge.ai>
Cc: Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>, Priyom Das <priyom.das@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 27 Apr 2026 09:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Reserved GPU pool — token economics & throughput check

Hi Emma — appreciate the fast confirmation and the detail. I pulled Priyom and Vanessa into a short mutual action plan below and included a quick straw-man sizing (two paths) we can validate against a 5-week token log if you share it.

Mutual action plan (next 7 business days):
- BrightBridge: share 5-week token log or a CSV export of token volumes by hour (ACTION: Emma) — target by Wed, Apr 29.
- Redwood (Priyom): run the capacity mapping and kernel-level profiling (A100 / H100) with your model archetype + sequence distribution — deliver by Thu, Apr 30.
- Redwood (Vanessa): prepare an updated reserved quote that includes both a cost-optimized A100 option and a latency-optimized H100 option, with explicit $/1M token estimates and burst-handling options — deliver by Fri, May 1.

Straw-man sizing (conservative assumptions):
Option A — Cost-optimized (A100):
- GPUs: 8 x A100-80GB (reserved), sustained throughput estimate ~900 tokens/sec aggregate, median latency ~300ms short-chat, $/1M tokens estimate: $165

Option B — Latency-optimized (H100):
- GPUs: 6 x H100-80GB (reserved), sustained throughput estimate ~1,100 tokens/sec aggregate, median latency ~220ms short-chat, $/1M tokens estimate: $205

Burst handling recommendation:
- Use a small permanent reserve sized for baseline plus an autoscale/burst policy that allows ephemeral lanes to spin up on top of the reserve. This keeps committed cost lower while managing campaign peaks. We’ll include an example lane sizing in the quote.

Attachments added: capacity_sizing_notes.txt (assumptions & perf knobs)

If those straw numbers look directionally right, Vanessa will formalize the quote and Priyom will run a quick perf test against a sample of your token log.

Best,
Ben Carter
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com

----
